title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracking framework specifically designed for object tracking within sparse 3D point cloud environments. The primary contributions are twofold: first, the development of a shape-aware feature learning network aimed at improving target discrimination within the search area; and second, the creation of a voxel-to-BEV target localization network intended to enhance the precision of 3D object localization. The proposed methodology achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods, with comprehensive ablation studies effectively demonstrating the efficacy of its various design elements.

Main Review:
This submission addresses a highly relevant and challenging problem: robust object tracking in 3D point clouds. The manuscript is exceptionally clear and well-structured, making it straightforward for readers to follow. It provides ample technical detail, ensuring the proposed methodology is thoroughly explained. Fundamentally, the approach appears technically sound, and the motivation behind its various design choices is clearly articulated. While not every claim is exhaustively justified, the experimental results do provide substantial support for many of the paper's assertions.

The presented method is genuinely novel, and its distinctions from prior works are effectively highlighted and discussed. The related work section is comprehensive and appropriate for the scope of the research. A particularly strong aspect of this submission is its impressive overall experimental performance, which demonstrates a significant margin of improvement over existing tracking methods.

However, certain claims made in the paper lack complete experimental substantiation. For instance, the authors suggest their method is superior at handling sparse point clouds compared to benchmarks such as SC3D and P2B. Yet, a comparison between Table 1 and Table 2 reveals a counterintuitive trend: when the dataset transitions from encompassing all object types to one specifically focused on sparse objects, only the proposed method experiences a performance decrement. Conversely, both baseline methods actually show an improvement. This discrepancy raises questions about whether the claimed "shape-aware feature" genuinely renders the proposed method more adept at processing sparse point clouds.

Furthermore, the paper does not include ablation studies that explicitly justify the necessity of incorporating both global and local features within two key components: the template feature embedding module and the shape-aware feature learning module. The absence of such analysis makes it difficult to fully assess the individual contribution and importance of this dual-feature approach.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As previously noted, some of the paper's assertions require further empirical validation. Specifically, the claims regarding enhanced performance with sparse point clouds and the essential role of combining local and global features in both the template feature embedding and shape-aware feature learning modules need more rigorous experimental backing. Additionally, the scope of the ablation study is somewhat limited, focusing solely on the 'car' category. To truly substantiate the claimed contributions and demonstrate their generalizability, it would be highly beneficial to extend these ablation studies to encompass all four object categories. I strongly encourage the authors to address these points during the rebuttal phase.

A minor textual point: some phrasing appears slightly awkward, such as in lines 378-380, where "stable does not mean accurate" could be rephrased for greater clarity or precision. Finally, it would be important for the paper to explicitly acknowledge its potential limitations, particularly that the proposed BEV-based object proposal mechanism might not be suitable for indoor environments, where a bird's-eye view representation may be insufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
The paper introduces a 3D object tracking system designed to pinpoint a target object within a designated search volume, given a reference template. The proposed architecture primarily comprises two key components: a Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and a Voxel-to-Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) target localization module. The feature learning network processes both the template and search space point clouds, generating a feature embedding for the search area that emphasizes its correspondence with the template and its capacity for dense point cloud reconstruction. This resulting search space feature is then fed into the Voxel-to-BEV localization network, which projects the 3D point clouds into a 2D BEV representation for subsequent target identification. The authors demonstrate that their method achieves a notable performance improvement over existing state-of-the-art approaches.

**Main Review:**
Overall, this submission presents a method that demonstrably surpasses prior work in both performance metrics and computational efficiency. However, I hold several reservations regarding the clarity of the writing, the degree of novelty in certain components, and the depth of the conducted ablation studies.

On the positive side, the empirical results unequivocally show that the proposed methodology outperforms current state-of-the-art techniques. The paper establishes a robust baseline for comparison, showcases enhanced performance, and includes a partial ablation analysis that validates the utility of the introduced modules. Furthermore, a thorough examination of computational costs is provided, which is crucial for assessing its practical applicability in real-world scenarios. I found the insight particularly intriguing that a point cloud generation task, exclusively employed during the training phase, contributes positively to the expressive power of the point cloud features used for localization.

Nevertheless, I would like to raise a few points of concern:

Firstly, the manuscript's writing quality is subpar. It contains an excessive number of evident grammatical errors and sentences that appear unpolished. A comprehensive proofreading effort is imperative to improve the readability and professionalism of this paper.

Secondly, the "Template Feature Embedding Networks" module appears to be an overly intricate construction, heavily reliant on engineering sophisticated features without sufficient supporting ablation studies to justify its complexity. While the rationale behind certain design choices is discernible, these details are not adequately substantiated by a rigorous comparative analysis. Especially considering the typical expectations for theoretical grounding at this conference venue, it is challenging to fully appreciate such a heavily engineered solution without more robust empirical or theoretical validation. For instance, an ablation study exploring the impact of global versus local features, or comparing different distance functions used for correlation and similarity mapping, would significantly enhance the understanding and credibility of this module.

Thirdly, the "Voxel-to-BEV target localization network" seems to draw heavily from existing literature in 3D object detection. I struggle to identify this as a novel contribution. The paper should explicitly acknowledge and cite foundational works such as PointPillar and SECOND, as these are widely recognized 3D object detection encoders whose architectural principles bear a striking resemblance to the module presented here.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The paper lacks a comprehensive discussion of its inherent limitations or potential societal implications. While some limited analysis of failure cases is provided in the supplementary material, for a tracking problem of this nature, I believe a more thorough examination of limitations such as re-identification challenges and tracklet initialization complexities is warranted. Additionally, the conference guidelines typically expect at least a brief statement addressing the societal impact of the work, which is currently absent.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This submission introduces a novel Siamese voxel2BEV object tracking framework specifically designed for autonomous driving scenarios. The proposed architectural design demonstrates commendable resilience when processing sparse point cloud data, leading to substantial improvements in tracking performance. Empirical evaluations conducted on the well-established KITTI benchmarks strongly affirm the efficacy of the methodology presented.

**Main Review:**

**Strengths:**
*   The paper addresses a highly pertinent and challenging problem within the domain of autonomous driving, and the algorithmic design chosen represents a sound and appropriate solution for this task.
*   The experimental outcomes on the KITTI dataset are particularly impressive, showcasing a notable performance gain that significantly surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods.
*   The overall clarity of the paper's presentation is good, and the visual aids provided are very effective in helping readers intuitively grasp the core concepts behind the design.
*   A valuable contribution would be the release of the source code, as indicated in the supplementary materials. Given this is an application-focused work, making the code publicly available would greatly benefit and foster subsequent research efforts in the field.

**Weaknesses:**
*   The observed degradation in performance specifically for the 'cyclist' category points to potential limitations within the current approach. While the authors attribute this to a scarcity of training data, it raises a critical question: would a similar performance trend emerge if other object categories also suffered from insufficient training examples? This warrants further exploration or discussion.
*   As this paper focuses on object tracking, incorporating video demonstrations of the tracking outputs would significantly enhance the reader's understanding and intuition, proving more illustrative than static visualizations, even those provided in the supplementary material.
*   Minor point: Could the authors clarify if the color palette utilized in Figure 3 carries any specific semantic meaning? Upon initial inspection, I attempted to discern a pattern by mapping similar colors to distinct sections but was ultimately unsuccessful.
*   Minor point: The evaluation is exclusively conducted on the KITTI dataset. Furthermore, certain stages of the proposed pipeline necessitate parameter calibration (e.g., coefficients for loss functions). It would be beneficial if the authors could provide some general guidelines for parameter tuning when adapting the algorithm to novel datasets. How do the authors envision the proposed method's generalization capabilities to new datasets or real-world autonomous driving conditions?

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
Regarding broader implications, a key question arises concerning the project's scope: while acknowledging the focus on 3D object tracking using LiDAR input, for safety-critical applications such as autonomous driving, why not integrate supplementary RGB information when available? My hypothesis is that the current algorithm could potentially be extended to fuse RGB and LiDAR inputs, and a discussion from the authors on this possibility would be highly insightful.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

Summary:
This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.

Main Review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Summary:**

This manuscript introduces a novel approach for single-object tracking within point cloud data. The core methodology employs a tracking-by-detection framework, utilizing a Siamese network architecture to extract features from both the target template and the designated search area. To enhance the discriminative power of these features, the template feature is embedded into the search area feature, and the network is trained with a regularization loss focused on shape completion. For target localization, the paper deviates from conventional proposal-based methods, opting instead for a dense detection strategy that directly identifies the target within dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) voxel locations. Empirical evaluations conducted on a custom split of the KITTI dataset indicate significant performance improvements over existing state-of-the-art techniques.

**Main Review:**

While the paper presents a promising direction, several aspects warrant improvement to strengthen the submission:

1.  **Elucidation of Comparisons with Prior Work:**
    The proposed method draws heavily on two established techniques: P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. Specifically, the overarching two-stage architecture, which involves embedding the template feature into the search area feature via a Siamese network in the first step, followed by target localization, closely mirrors the design of P2B. The primary distinction from P2B lies in the second stage, where this work adopts a dense detection mechanism in contrast to P2B's proposal-based strategy, a change demonstrated to yield superior results, particularly for objects represented by sparse point clouds. To further highlight these advantages, I recommend supplementing the quantitative data in Table 5 with qualitative visualizations that specifically showcase these improvements.
    Similarly, the claimed novelty of "shape-aware feature learning" appears to largely build upon the scene completion regularization loss introduced in SC3D, with the main difference being the specific input features used (as described in Lines 187-190). To justify these modifications and underscore their technical novelty, a dedicated ablation study investigating the specific changes made to SC3D's regularization loss would be highly valuable. Overall, authors should provide a more explicit and detailed comparison with technically related previous methods, clearly delineating which components are inherited and which represent novel contributions designed to address specific existing challenges.

2.  **Clarity and Structure of the Methodology Section:**
    The current presentation of the methodology section is challenging to follow. It introduces a substantial amount of technical detail without sufficient contextual background or clear motivation for the architectural design choices. I strongly recommend a comprehensive revision and restructuring of this section in the updated submission to improve its readability and logical flow.

3.  **Expansion of Experimental Results:**
    The experimental section could benefit from additional results to offer a more thorough understanding of the model's behavior and performance characteristics. This includes, but is not limited to:
    *   The ablation study concerning SC3D, as suggested in point 1.
    *   A more comprehensive qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B across all four object categories. This is particularly important for the 'cyclist' category, where the proposed method appears to perform significantly worse compared to SC3D, and an explanation or further analysis is needed.
    *   Regarding the speed comparison in Table 3, a notable discrepancy exists: P2B is officially reported to achieve 40 FPS on an NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, yet this paper reports 17 FPS on a more powerful NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU. An explanation for this considerable performance gap is required.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**

I did not find specific discussions regarding the inherent limitations of the proposed method or its potential negative societal implications within the submission. I suggest including these important considerations in the revised manuscript.

**Rating:**

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

The results  look promising.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This manuscript introduces a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker, which integrates several distinct modules. These include mechanisms for global and local template matching, a network dedicated to shape completion, and a specialized voxel-to-BEV network for target localization.

**Main Review:**
The experimental outcomes presented in the paper appear encouraging, showcasing promising performance for the proposed tracking framework.

However, a primary reservation concerns the somewhat restricted novelty of the methodology. The core approach seems to be an amalgamation of various existing and well-known components. For instance, the template matching aspect bears a resemblance to techniques found in works like SiamRPN [1], while the combination of shape completion and BEV localization echoes principles observed in methods such as LaserNet [2]. While the integration of these established elements may be effective, the overall conceptual advancement might be considered incremental.

To further bolster the paper's scientific contribution and provide deeper insights into the method's internal workings, it would be highly beneficial to include comprehensive ablation studies. Specifically, an analysis that isolates and quantifies the individual contributions of the global and local template features is strongly desired. This would help elucidate their respective impacts on the tracker's performance.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors have adequately addressed the limitations of their work and considered its potential societal implications.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This submission introduces a 3D single-object tracking methodology leveraging point cloud data. To address challenges in sparse point cloud environments, the authors integrate a shape completion technique, aimed at enhancing feature learning capabilities. The proposed approach's performance is assessed using the KITTI dataset, where it reportedly surpasses several baseline methods.

**Main Review:**

**Strengths:**

1.  The design choices for crafting local and global feature embeddings, particularly for template feature extraction, are well-conceived and intuitive. The ablation studies presented in Table 4 convincingly demonstrate that these engineering efforts yield significant performance improvements.
2.  The qualitative results provided, especially those concerning sparse point clouds, offer compelling visual evidence of the method's practical utility and effectiveness in challenging scenarios.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  The overall utility and impact of the paper's primary contributions remain somewhat ambiguous based on the experimental evidence provided:

    a)  **Regarding Shape Completion:** The paper highlights a key distinction from prior work, specifically [13], by performing shape completion within the search area rather than on the template. While Table 4's ablation clearly shows a performance degradation without shape information from completion, a crucial comparison is missing: the proposed approach against shape completion applied to the template, as in [13]. Without this direct comparison, it's difficult to ascertain whether the authors' novel application of shape completion is genuinely superior or inferior to existing strategies. Furthermore, the absolute performance figures in Table 1 are not sufficient to resolve this, as differing codebases introduce numerous confounding variables. To provide a clearer assessment, I suggest either integrating template-based shape completion into the proposed method for a direct comparison, or modifying [13]'s codebase to incorporate search-area completion. Additionally, the intuitive rationale behind whether completing the template versus the search area (or potentially both) would yield differential benefits is not thoroughly elucidated.

    b)  **Regarding Voxel-to-BEV Target Localization:** The proposed method simplifies 3D voxel features into a 2D representation by applying a max-pooling operation along the z-axis. It is counter-intuitive how this process, which inherently discards information, could enhance the subsequent network's ability (specifically the z-axis head) to accurately localize the z-axis center. Conventional understanding suggests that such a downsampling step would complicate, rather than facilitate, precise z-axis estimation. This stands in contradiction to the paper's assertion (line 68) that this z-axis feature pooling leads to more effective and accurate regression for both 2D and z-axis centers. Moreover, a more detailed ablation specific to this voxel-to-BEV conversion is absent. For instance, an experiment demonstrating how the presence or absence of this step significantly influences the accuracy of both 2D and z-axis center estimations would be highly beneficial.

2.  **Network Architecture Justification:** The paper frequently references the use of PointNet++ (e.g., line 126) as a core component. Given that PointNet++ was introduced in NeurIPS 2017, making it four years old at the time of writing, the rationale for not employing more recent and potentially more advanced point cloud processing architectures is not adequately justified. The field has progressed considerably since then, and a discussion on why this particular older architecture was chosen over newer alternatives would strengthen the paper.

3.  **Evaluation Protocol Limitations:** The evaluation strategy, which relies on only two sequences from the KITTI dataset for testing, raises significant concerns about the robustness and generalizability of the findings. The entire KITTI tracking dataset is already considered relatively small and often insufficient for comprehensive and robust evaluation in the current research landscape, let alone a mere two sequences. While I acknowledge the paper's adherence to prior work's evaluation protocol, I believe this protocol itself is no longer fully justified, especially given the availability of more extensive and robust benchmarks. Datasets such as nuScenes (with 150 validation and 150 test sequences) or Waymo offer substantially more data and object diversity for rigorous evaluation. I strongly recommend that the authors include an evaluation on at least one additional, larger-scale dataset, such as nuScenes or Waymo, unless there is a compelling and explicitly stated reason restricting the evaluation solely to these two KITTI sequences. Under the current limited evaluation, I lack confidence in the robustness and meaningfulness of any quantitative results or analyses, particularly for less frequent object categories like Van and Cyclist, which are likely to exhibit high variance due to insufficient examples within just two sequences.

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**
The paper adequately addresses its technical limitations. The discussion of potential negative societal impacts is omitted, which is deemed acceptable for this work.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

reviews.text:
**Summary:**
The authors introduce a novel framework, the Siamese Voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) Tracker, designed for localizing and tracking individual objects within sparse point cloud data. The proposed methodology involves feeding a template and a designated search region into a Siamese network to extract pertinent point features. Subsequently, it adaptively learns the correlation between these two inputs, embedding the template's features into potential objects within the search area. A key component is its ability to learn shape-aware features, which is achieved by generating a dense point cloud from the initial sparse input to better characterize object geometry. The approach further refines 3D object localization by decomposing the object's 3D center into a 2D planar coordinate and a separate z-axis component, which are then regressed within a dense BEV representation. The paper asserts that this framework establishes new state-of-the-art performance on the KITTI tracking benchmark.

**Main Review:**

1.  **Clarity and Scope:** While the paper is generally well-structured and the technical concepts are presented with reasonable clarity, allowing for an easy understanding of the proposed methodology, a significant ambiguity persists regarding the *specific application domain* of the technique. The core assertion is that this is a 3D object tracking framework. However, it remains unclear whether the system is designed for single-object tracking (SOT) or multi-object tracking (MOT). Crucially, the described framework appears to lack fundamental components typically associated with tracking systems, such as mechanisms for track initiation, termination, or handling occlusions. From the detailed exposition, the approach seems to function more akin to a 3D object detection method rather than a comprehensive tracking paradigm. The paper omits vital explanations regarding how this framework can be extended or adapted for either single or multiple object tracking scenarios. Furthermore, given the use of the KITTI dataset for evaluation, the justification for its application in a single-object tracking context, and how its performance is benchmarked against existing methods on the KITTI leaderboards, is not convincingly articulated.

2.  **Insufficient Related Work and Comparisons:** The literature review and the selection of comparative frameworks appear notably limited and somewhat selective. The paper overlooks a substantial body of relevant work within the 2D multi-object tracking (MOT) community, including prominent benchmarks like MOTChallenge and existing KITTI 2D MOT methods. Similarly, for 3D MOT, numerous state-of-the-art approaches evaluated on diverse datasets such as KITTI, NuScenes, Waymo, and JRDB are absent from the discussion and experimental comparison. To provide a few examples of such critical omissions:
    *   Weng, X., Wang, J., Held, D., & Kitani, K. (IROS 2020). *3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics.* (Relevant for KITTI, JRDB, NuScenes).
    *   Shenoi, A., Patel, M., Gwak, J., Goebel, P., Sadeghian, A., Rezatofighi, H., Martin-Martin, R., & Savarese, S. (IROS 2020). *JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset.* (Relevant for KITTI, JRDB).
    *   Kim, A., Ošep, A., & Leal-Taixé, L. (ICRA 2021). *EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion.* (Relevant for KITTI, NuScenes).

3.  **Contribution and Rigor:** The paper primarily presents an applied solution without introducing significant novel theoretical contributions. The proposed system appears to be an application-focused endeavor, leveraging existing machine learning tools with some incremental modifications and extensions. While the NeurIPS community does value high-quality application papers, such contributions typically necessitate a rigorous and extensive experimental validation. This includes comprehensive evaluations across multiple diverse benchmark datasets and thorough comparisons against a wide array of state-of-the-art frameworks to unequivocally demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed system. In its current form, lacking this level of empirical validation, I find myself reluctant to endorse its acceptance for publication in this venue.

4.  **Experimental Evaluation and Dataset Scope:** The experimental validation is confined solely to the KITTI dataset. A critical concern is the use of a novel evaluation metric, which deviates from the established and widely accepted metrics (e.g., MOTA, HOTA) typically employed by the KITTI benchmark for multi-object tracking. It is imperative to understand why the approach was not evaluated on the official KITTI test set using these standard, widely-recognized tracking metrics. Furthermore, the paper does not clarify how the framework would effectively handle non-rigid objects, such as humans, particularly concerning the definition and adaptation of the 'template' in such dynamic scenarios. If the system possesses the capability to track non-rigid objects, its absence of evaluation on relevant 3D human tracking datasets like JRDB is a notable omission. Finally, the scope of evaluation is too narrow; the efficacy of the proposed method remains unverified on other prominent 3D tracking datasets relevant to autonomous driving, such as NuScenes or Waymo.

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**
The authors have unfortunately neglected to include a discussion on the limitations of their proposed framework or its potential societal implications. While the tracking of 3D objects in point clouds holds substantial promise for critical applications like autonomous transportation and self-driving vehicles, it is equally important to acknowledge the broader implications. Tracking technologies, in general, can be readily adapted for human monitoring and surveillance purposes, thereby raising significant ethical and privacy concerns that warrant explicit consideration and discussion.

**Rating:**
**4: Ok but not good enough - rejection**

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This manuscript investigates the generalization performance of the MAML algorithm across two distinct meta-testing scenarios: one where new tasks align with the training distribution, and another involving entirely novel, unseen tasks. The analysis is conducted under specific conditions, namely, a (strongly) convex loss function and a single-step SGD adaptation process within MAML. For the former scenario, the authors introduce a novel stability concept, which enables the derivation of a generalization error bound sensitive to the number of data points available per task. In the latter, the work establishes a generalization error bound leveraging the total variation distance between the distributions of new tasks and those encountered during training.

**Main Review:**

**Originality & Significance:**
This paper offers notable advancements over prior research, introducing several insightful theoretical contributions. A key strength lies in the development of a novel stability measure, which is instrumental in formulating a generalization error bound. This new bound demonstrates a crucial dependency on the number of data points available per task, leading to a tighter theoretical guarantee when compared to existing frameworks. Furthermore, the authors present an innovative methodology utilizing total variation distance to analyze generalization bounds in scenarios where test-time tasks originate from distributions distinct from those seen during training.

**Quality:**
While the theoretical advancements are appreciated, two primary limitations in the analysis warrant discussion. Firstly, the reliance on a convex loss function is a significant assumption, given that MAML is frequently applied to deep neural networks characterized by non-convex loss landscapes. Consequently, the direct applicability and implications of these findings for practical MAML deployments remain somewhat ambiguous. It is commendable, however, that the authors explicitly address and justify this limitation in Remark 2. Secondly, the analysis is constrained to a single SGD step during the MAML adaptation phase. The paper would benefit from a more comprehensive discussion regarding the implications or challenges that arise when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation process extends beyond this single iteration.

Overall, the manuscript is well-organized and clearly written. I found only a few minor editorial points that could be addressed:
*   In Assumption 1(ii), please verify the wording around 'unbounded'.
*   On Line 196, it appears that the citation 'from [1]' should potentially be 'from [24]'.
*   Line 218 contains a typo; 'deferentially private' should be corrected to 'differentially private'.
*   In Lines 286 and 287, the variable 'k' should consistently be 'K'.
*   The title of Section 3.3 has 'Task' capitalized, which might be inconsistent with other section titles.

**Rating:**
Despite the analytical assumptions, the paper makes valuable theoretical contributions to understanding MAML's generalization properties, particularly through its novel stability notion and use of total variation distance. The work is largely sound and well-presented. Therefore, I recommend acceptance.

**Recommendation:** 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.
