reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper addresses the question of zero-shot or fast generalization of value-based deep reinforcement learning agents using visual inputs to image transformation which do not change the (conceptual) state of the environment. The long term goal is therefore training agents which learn efficient, high return control policies both which are robust to irrelevant variations in high dimensional input observations. The ultimate goal seems to be generalization of learned skills to novel environments, but as far as I can tell this particular evaluation philosophy is implemented in experiments.

The paper reviews previous attempts to learn robust state representations to visual variations and formulates clear hypotheses for explaining the causes of difficulties which prior methods faced and were hindered by. The said hypotheses suggest a simplified approach (SVEA) which is empirically developed and evaluated in the remainder of the paper. Control tasks from widely used benchmarks are used to evaluate the method, somewhat less usual is the focus on vision-based high-dimensional versions of such tasks, instead of standard domains such as Atari games and benchmarks for generalization such as Natural Atari [1], CoinRun/ProcGen [2].

Main Review:
I am generally happy with the paper, I have a few suggestions:

* The goal of reinforcement learning is discovering an optimal control policy, not necessarily an action-value function. Strictly speaking, value estimation is just a crutch. Hence, why bother with robust value estimation? Why not learn value functions without augmentations, use them to discover an optimal policy, and then use supervised learning [3, 4] to encode a robust policy? Please clarify in the text! The proposed method is attempting to solve a problem (RL under observation augmentation) which may not be relevant if all that is needed is good control under diverse observation augmentations. How am I getting this wrong?
* Indeed, for all these environments it is not clear that value prediction from vision is needed at all, since a policy could be derived much faster from full state representations in terms of sample efficiency and computational speed, and then have that policy supervise a vision to action mapping, circumventing the robust value estimation problem altogether.
* There are many possible definitions of generalization in reinforcement learning; one is accurate value prediction in unseen states, as realistic problems have enormous state spaces; another is robust value prediction from high-dimensional observations, where states may have been seem before but there is extraneous variance in the observations which needs to be dealt with. Finally, there are progressively stronger definitions of generalization, namely across domain gaps which may or may not introduce variability relevant to the control policy itself. Which definition have you tested for in your experiments?
* Could you please clarify in the main text which experiments use some form of state information, e.g. "proprioceptive" data common in control tasks, as well as target object information, and which do not? If such information is used, how does it reflect on the ability to extrapolate the presented results to problems where such information is not available.
* Could you please specify for each set of results whether the set of initial states was randomized and by how much? One surefire way to get robustness to high variability in observations is open-loop control, which can also be implemented by non-recurrent architectures, e.g. by relying heavily on proprioceptive inputs or general statistics of observations. 


References:
1. (Natural Atari) Amy Zhang et al. 2018. Natural Environment Benchmarks for Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.06032
2. (CoinRun/ProcGen) Karl Cobbe et al. 2019. Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.01588
3. Andrei Rusu et al. 2015. Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1511.06295
4. Wojciech Czarnecki et al. 2019. Distilling Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.02186

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I understand why every paper claims the maximal general applicability possible which cannot be refuted by reviewers without experiments; please note that proving negatives is hard, and much rests on your honesty and expertise alone. I am not comfortable with text which talks about aspirational goals as if they were achieved in this paper. The abstract talks about generalization to novel environments and great improvements in stability and sample efficiency, it seems to me that not enough attention to detail has been expended in quantifying the limitations of this approach and the wider family of algorithms to which it belongs. For example, a claim of "greatly improved sample efficiency" sounds outstanding, but then it is not clear to a non-RL specialist how many order of magnitude we are talking about, and how augmentations themselves make the problem worse. While the paper deserves credit for exploring this subject in detail, such claims are vague at best and may not age well; sample efficiency may mean very different amounts of experience in 5 years time.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper investigates the challenge of achieving zero-shot or rapid generalization in value-based deep reinforcement learning agents that process visual inputs. The focus is on scenarios where image transformations do not alter the underlying conceptual state of the environment. The broader objective appears to be the development of control policies that are both efficient and yield high returns, while also being robust to irrelevant variations in complex, high-dimensional observational data. While the ultimate aspiration seems to be the generalization of acquired skills to entirely novel environments, the specific experimental methodology employed primarily addresses the robustness aspect.

The authors provide a comprehensive overview of prior endeavors to learn robust state representations against visual variability. They then articulate clear hypotheses to explain the difficulties encountered by previous methods, which subsequently informed the development of their simplified approach, SVEA. This method is empirically developed and evaluated throughout the paper. The evaluation utilizes control tasks from well-established benchmarks, distinguishing itself by concentrating on vision-based, high-dimensional versions of these tasks, rather than conventional domains like Atari games or specialized generalization benchmarks such as Natural Atari [1] or CoinRun/ProcGen [2].

**Main Review:**
Overall, I find the paper to be a valuable contribution, and I am generally satisfied with its content. I do, however, have several points that I believe warrant further clarification or discussion:

*   The primary objective of reinforcement learning is to discover an optimal control policy, rather than strictly an action-value function, which can often serve as an intermediate step or "crutch." Given this, I question the emphasis on robust value estimation itself. An alternative strategy might involve learning value functions without observation augmentations, using these to identify an optimal policy, and then employing supervised learning techniques (e.g., policy distillation [3, 4]) to encode a robust policy. Could the authors please elaborate in the text why their proposed method, which tackles RL under observation augmentation, is the most pertinent approach if the ultimate goal is simply robust control under diverse observation variations? I would appreciate a clearer explanation of where my understanding might be incorrect.
*   Following from the above, it is not immediately clear for the environments studied why value prediction from visual inputs is necessary at all. A policy could potentially be derived with significantly greater sample and computational efficiency from full state representations. This policy could then be used to supervise a mapping from vision to action, thereby circumventing the entire problem of robust value estimation from high-dimensional visual inputs.
*   Generalization in reinforcement learning encompasses various definitions. One definition refers to accurate value prediction in previously unseen states, which is critical given the immense size of realistic state spaces. Another involves robust value prediction from high-dimensional observations, where the underlying states might have been encountered before, but the observations themselves contain extraneous variance that needs to be managed. Finally, there are progressively stronger forms of generalization, such as those across significant domain gaps, which may or may not introduce variability directly relevant to the control policy itself. It would be beneficial if the authors could explicitly clarify which specific definition of generalization their experiments are designed to test.
*   To enhance clarity, could the authors specify within the main text which experiments incorporate any form of state information (e.g., "proprioceptive" data commonly found in control tasks, or target object information) and which rely solely on visual inputs? If such auxiliary information is utilized, a discussion on how its presence might influence the generalizability of the presented results to problems where such information is unavailable would be valuable.
*   For each set of experimental results, please clarify whether the initial states were randomized, and if so, to what extent. It is important to consider that robustness to high variability in observations can sometimes be achieved through simpler mechanisms, such as open-loop control, which can be implemented by non-recurrent architectures that heavily rely on proprioceptive inputs or general statistical properties of observations.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
I recognize the common practice in academic papers to claim the broadest possible applicability, which can be challenging for reviewers to refute without conducting their own experiments. However, demonstrating negative claims is inherently difficult, and therefore, a significant degree of responsibility rests on the authors' candor and expertise. I am uncomfortable with sections of the text that present aspirational goals as if they have already been achieved within the scope of this paper. For instance, the abstract mentions "generalization to novel environments" and "great improvements in stability and sample efficiency." It appears that insufficient attention has been paid to rigorously quantifying the limitations of this specific approach and the broader family of algorithms it belongs to. The claim of "greatly improved sample efficiency," while outstanding, lacks specific quantification; it is unclear to a non-RL specialist what order of magnitude improvement is implied, or how the use of augmentations might itself exacerbate the problem. While the paper deserves credit for its detailed exploration of this subject, such broad claims are, at best, vague and may not withstand the test of time, as the definition and benchmarks for "sample efficiency" are likely to evolve significantly within the next five years.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper presents a way of improving training stability of DrQ (an image-based RL algorithm that uses data augmentation) by applying image augmentation more carefully. The authors propose to input a mix of augmented and un-augmented observations into the critic, and stop augmenting the actor's inputs. On the considered benchmarks the proposed method demonstrates better generalization results.
reviews.main_review:
The paper identifies an issue with the increased variance of the target estimation in DrQ (image-based RL with data augmentation). The authors conjecture two root causes for the increased variance: non-deterministic Q-targets and over-regularization.
To remedy these issues they propose to apply data augmentation more carefully. Specifically, the authors suggest to mix augmented and un-augmented inputs to the critic and stop augmenting of the actor's inputs. This method is then empirically justified in a series of experiment on tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite.

Pros:
* The author investigate an interesting question of an effect of data augmentation on generalization in image-based RL. 
* The method is thoroughly verified in different settings. 
* The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.
* The exploration of alternative architecture, such as ViT.

Cons:
* The paper proposes a small trick that lacks in novelty. The impact of data-augmentation on the estimation variance was also studied in the original DrQ paper (Kostrikov et. al), where a very similar technique was proposed -- the target estimation was done over 2 different augmentations. This work introduces a tiny modification of the DrQ's variance reduction approach, that just combines one augmented and one un-augmented observation instead of combining two augmented observations. Furthermore, the authors fail to mention this prior result in their paper and compare to it. 
* From the paper it is not clear what hyper-parameters are used for the baselines, specifically for DrQ. If the authors use the original DrQ's parameters, then I see several major issues with the empirical results: 1) SVEA uses encoder arch that consists of 11 conv layers, while DrQ only uses 4. 2) The size of replay buffer of SVEA is 500K, while DrQ's 100K. 3) SVEA virtually increases the batch size by factor of 2, which usually leads to better performance on the DeepMind Control Suite tasks, it is not clear if the authors increase batch size for DrQ or not. These discrepancies alone could result in a drastic difference in performance and can render the empirical study to be invalid. 
* There is no experiments that compare individual components of SVEA to verify is that the improved performance comes from the proposed method or better/different hyper-parameters. 
* The authors claim in line 30 that they "theoretically ground" their findings, but this is not the case as there is no theoretical results presented. In general, the authors don't do a good job justifying that the two pitfalls are real. Their argument is hand-wavy and is based on a series of indirect comparisons to the baseline (DrQ). Unfortunately, there are too many moving parts in those comparisons that make it virtually impossible to validate the claim (see also the point above about the hyper-parameter discrepancies).

Given the very limited scope of novelty and technical contribution, the failure to mention the prior work that is almost identical, and the inconclusive empirical study I recommend to reject this paper from a high-profile venue of NeurIPS.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.text:
**Summary:**
This paper introduces an approach aimed at enhancing the training stability of DrQ, an image-based Reinforcement Learning algorithm that leverages data augmentation. The authors propose a more judicious application of image augmentation, specifically by feeding a blend of augmented and un-augmented observations to the critic network, while ceasing augmentation for the actor's inputs. Empirical results on the DeepMind Control Suite benchmarks suggest that this method leads to improved generalization.

**Main Review:**
The paper addresses a recognized challenge in DrQ: the elevated variance in target estimation, a consequence of using data augmentation in image-based RL. The authors hypothesize that this increased variance stems from two primary sources: non-deterministic Q-targets and excessive regularization. To mitigate these issues, they advocate for a more refined application of data augmentation. Their proposed solution involves supplying the critic with a mixture of both augmented and un-augmented inputs, and crucially, refraining from augmenting the actor's inputs. This methodology is then empirically validated through a series of experiments conducted on tasks within the DeepMind Control Suite.

**Strengths:**
*   The research delves into a compelling and relevant question concerning the effect of data augmentation on generalization performance in image-based Reinforcement Learning.
*   The proposed methodology undergoes comprehensive empirical verification across various experimental setups.
*   The paper is written with commendable clarity and is easy to comprehend.
*   The inclusion of investigations into alternative architectures, such as Vision Transformers (ViT), is a positive aspect.

**Weaknesses:**
*   The core contribution of this paper appears to be a modest adjustment lacking significant novelty. The impact of data augmentation on estimation variance was previously investigated in the original DrQ paper (Kostrikov et al.), where a very similar technique was proposed: performing target estimation over two distinct augmentations. This current work offers only a minor modification to DrQ's existing variance reduction approach, by combining one augmented and one un-augmented observation instead of two augmented ones. Furthermore, the authors fail to cite this crucial prior work or include it in their comparative analysis.
*   The empirical study suffers from a lack of clarity regarding the hyper-parameters used for baselines, particularly for DrQ. If the authors relied on the original DrQ parameters, several major inconsistencies arise that could invalidate the results: 1) The SVEA encoder architecture uses 11 convolutional layers, whereas DrQ typically uses only 4. 2) SVEA's replay buffer size is 500K, significantly larger than DrQ's 100K. 3) SVEA effectively doubles its batch size, a factor generally known to boost performance on DeepMind Control Suite tasks, yet it's not clear whether DrQ's batch size was similarly adjusted. Such discrepancies alone could lead to substantial performance differences and render the comparative empirical study unreliable.
*   The paper does not include experiments that isolate the individual components of SVEA, making it impossible to verify whether the observed performance improvements are genuinely attributable to the proposed method or merely to the use of different/optimized hyper-parameters.
*   The authors' claim in line 30 of "theoretically grounding" their findings is unsubstantiated, as no theoretical results are presented. More broadly, the justification for the two identified pitfalls (non-deterministic Q-targets and over-regularization) is weak. Their argument is largely qualitative and based on a series of indirect comparisons to the DrQ baseline. Unfortunately, the numerous confounding variables in these comparisons, stemming from the aforementioned hyper-parameter discrepancies, make it virtually impossible to validate these claims rigorously.

**Overall Assessment:**
Given the very limited scope of novelty and technical contribution, the failure to acknowledge and compare against prior work that presents an almost identical solution, and the inconclusive nature of the empirical study due to significant methodological inconsistencies, I recommend rejecting this paper from a high-profile venue such as NeurIPS.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors have adequately addressed these sections.

**Rating:**
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: On the Value of Infinite Gradients in Variational Autoencoder Models

reviews.summary: The paper investigates a specific work regime for VAE characterized by close to infinite gradients. The authors states that this phenomenon occurs for small values of a regularization power $\gamma$ and cannot be avoided, if one wants a perfect data reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:
- Clear analysis of presented phenomenon with a theorem, nice illustrations, profound investigation.

- Present explanation of a well-known effect during the VAE training.

Cons:
- The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice it can be the case, that infinite gradients phenomenon occurs due to different reasons, so there is no guarantee that the paper finally solves the problem.

- Optimization of gamma refers to Bayesian problem statement, thus a full Bayesian discussion of the problem at hand can provide more intuition on what is going on, and how can we avoid the infinite gradient effect with a proper prior on parameters.

- Formula (6) – why the objective function has h in the first term, as usually we don’t use any h() for the training of AE?
Maybe there exists another criterion for AE learning, that doesn’t require assumptions of Theorem 4 to get the perfect reconstruction with at most r nonzero rows of Z (for example, we impose only some regularisation on z in the second term in (6)?)? 
Under which conditions on data we get that $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ requires that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least r<d rows?

- Does the infinite gradients phenomenon occur in other types of models? What makes this statement unique? What is with GMM or Kernel PCA or bayesian regression or PCA? E.g., in case of kernel PCA we can also require the data interpolation property. Will we observe the same problem?

- Assumption in formula (4) seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAE and for statistics in general. It can lead to bad solutions making further arguments invalid. A discussion is needed.
Do we observe the infinite gradients phenomenon in case we do not require the interpolation property?

- The present work uses insights from 
B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018.
I would expect some discussion on the relation of the new results and the results from that paper.

- I understand that in case of sufficiently clean data, lying on the manifold, the interpolation property is a reasonable requirement. According to the authors, we have to tune gamma to achieve it. Are there any specific algorithms for tuning gamma, not just general advices?

- The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if sigma_z is constant and gamma is small. Theorem 4 are proved for the optimization problem (6). I am OK to transfer the conclusions of Theorem 4 to the VAE case when gamma is small. But the requirement of constant sigma_z is not obvious. What does happen if we model sigma_z with some neural network and tune its parameters? Will we observe the same effects? Maybe adaptive sigma_z will compensate the instability?

In general, the paper is interesting, but the assumptions sometimes look too narrow or results are not formally connected, I need more clarifications.

Minor comments:
- Line 103 – should it be minimal instead of maximal?

- Line 325 - "spare representations". Maybe "sparse"?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: not applicable

Maybe there exists another criterion for AE learning, that doesn’t require assumptions of Theorem 4 to get the perfect reconstruction with at most r nonzero rows of Z (for example, we impose only some regularisation on z in the second term in (6)?)? 
Under which conditions on data we get that $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ requires that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least r<d rows?

- Does the infinite gradients phenomenon occur in other types of models? What makes this statement unique? What is with GMM or Kernel PCA or bayesian regression or PCA? E.g., in case of kernel PCA we can also require the data interpolation property. Will we observe the same problem?

- Assumption in formula (4) seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAE and for statistics in general. It can lead to bad solutions making further arguments invalid. A discussion is needed.
Do we observe the infinite gradients phenomenon in case we do not require the interpolation property?

- The present work uses insights from 
B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018.
I would expect some discussion on the relation of the new results and the results from that paper.

- I understand that in case of sufficiently clean data, lying on the manifold, the interpolation property is a reasonable requirement. According to the authors, we have to tune gamma to achieve it. Are there any specific algorithms for tuning gamma, not just general advices?

- The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if sigma_z is constant and gamma is small. Theorem 4 are proved for the optimization problem (6). I am OK to transfer the conclusions of Theorem 4 to the VAE case when gamma is small. But the requirement of constant sigma_z is not obvious. What does happen if we model sigma_z with some neural network and tune its parameters? Will we observe the same effects? Maybe adaptive sigma_z will compensate the instability?

In general, the paper is interesting, but the assumptions sometimes look too narrow or results are not formally connected, I need more clarifications.

Minor comments:
- Line 103 – should it be minimal instead of maximal?

- Line 325 - "spare representations". Maybe "sparse"?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
not applicable

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This manuscript examines a particular operational mode within Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) where gradients approach infinity. The authors posit that this phenomenon arises when the regularization parameter $\gamma$ is small and is an inherent consequence if perfect data reconstruction is desired.

**Strengths:**
*   The authors provide a lucid analysis of the identified phenomenon, supported by a formal theorem, compelling illustrations, and a thorough investigation.
*   The paper offers a valuable explanation for an effect that is commonly observed during the training process of VAE models.

**Weaknesses:**
*   A primary concern is that the paper attributes the occurrence of large gradients to a singular cause. In practical scenarios, various factors can lead to the phenomenon of infinite gradients, which implies that this work may not offer a comprehensive solution to the broader problem.
*   Given that the optimization of $\gamma$ is inherently linked to Bayesian problem formulations, a more extensive Bayesian treatment of the issue could significantly enhance understanding. Such a discussion, potentially involving the role of appropriate priors on parameters, might shed light on strategies to mitigate or avoid the infinite gradient effect.
*   Several clarifications are needed regarding Formula (6). Firstly, the inclusion of 'h' in the first term of the objective function is unusual, as autoencoder training typically does not involve such a component. Secondly, it would be beneficial to explore whether alternative criteria for AE learning exist that could achieve perfect reconstruction with at most 'r' nonzero rows of Z without relying on the specific assumptions of Theorem 4 (e.g., by only applying regularization to 'z' in the second term of (6)). Finally, the conditions under which the data reconstruction $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ necessitates that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least r<d rows should be explicitly stated and justified.
*   The discussion could benefit from addressing whether this infinite gradient phenomenon is exclusive to VAEs or if it manifests in other model architectures. The authors should elaborate on what makes this observation unique to their context. Consideration of models such as Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs), Kernel PCA, Bayesian regression, or standard PCA would be valuable. For instance, if a data interpolation property were imposed in Kernel PCA, would a similar problem arise?
*   Assumption (4) appears to be overly restrictive and potentially unrealistic, especially considering the inherent noisiness of data typically encountered in VAE applications and statistical modeling. This assumption could lead to suboptimal solutions and potentially undermine the validity of subsequent arguments. A thorough discussion on the implications of this assumption is warranted. Furthermore, it should be clarified whether the infinite gradient phenomenon persists when the interpolation property is not strictly enforced.
*   Given that the current work draws insights from the paper by B. Dai and D. Wipf (ICLR, 2018) titled 'Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models,' a dedicated discussion outlining the relationship between the novel findings presented here and the conclusions drawn in that prior publication is essential.
*   While the interpolation property seems justifiable for sufficiently clean data residing on a low-dimensional manifold, and the authors indicate that $\gamma$ requires tuning to achieve this, the paper currently lacks specific algorithmic recommendations for this tuning process. Beyond general guidance, are there concrete methods or algorithms proposed for optimizing $\gamma$?
*   The derivation of optimization problem (6) from the general VAE optimization problem (3) appears contingent on two conditions: a constant $\sigma_z$ and a small $\gamma$. While it is reasonable to extend the conclusions of Theorem 4 (proven for problem (6)) to the VAE case when $\gamma$ is small, the necessity of a constant $\sigma_z$ is not immediately evident. It would be valuable to explore the implications if $\sigma_z$ were instead modeled by a neural network with tunable parameters. Would the observed effects persist under such adaptive modeling of $\sigma_z$? Could an adaptive $\sigma_z$ potentially mitigate or compensate for the instability?

Overall, the paper presents an interesting investigation. However, the scope of some assumptions appears overly restrictive, and certain results lack clear formal connections. Further clarifications are needed to strengthen the arguments.

**Minor Comments:**
*   On Line 103, please verify if 'minimal' would be more appropriate than 'maximal'.
*   On Line 325, it appears 'spare representations' should likely be corrected to 'sparse representations'.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
Not Applicable

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary:
The paper investigates a specific work regime for VAE characterized by close to infinite gradients. The authors states that this phenomenon occurs for small values of a regularization power $\gamma$ and cannot be avoided, if one wants a perfect data reconstruction.

Main Review:
Pros:
- Clear analysis of presented phenomenon with a theorem, nice illustrations, profound investigation.

- Present explanation of a well-known effect during the VAE training.

Cons:
- The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice it can be the case, that infinite gradients phenomenon occurs due to different reasons, so there is no guarantee that the paper finally solves the problem.

- Optimization of gamma refers to Bayesian problem statement, thus a full Bayesian discussion of the problem at hand can provide more intuition on what is going on, and how can we avoid the infinite gradient effect with a proper prior on parameters.

- Formula (6) – why the objective function has h in the first term, as usually we don’t use any h() for the training of AE?

B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018.

- The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if sigma_z is constant and gamma is small. Theorem 4 are proved for the optimization problem (6). reviews.summary: This paper studies the infinite gradient of training VAEs, namely when the decoder's variance is a learnable parameter, the variance will converge to zero, resulting in unbounded gradient. Although previously this is seen as an undesirable property, the authors prove that unbounded gradients are necessary for obtaining optimal spare representations. The authors also try to argue that learning the variance is better than fixing it to an arbitrarily small value.
reviews.main_review:
The paper address an important issue in VAE training. It is widely observe that when the decoder variance is learnable, it will converge to a small number near zero. This paper make a nice connection between this phenomenon and optimal sparse representations, which is arguably the goal for learning an AE or VAE. By proving that it is inevitable to avoid the unbounded gradient while minimizing a generalization of AE loss, the adaptive decoder variance is justified.

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. When you study the learning of decoder variance, do you consider the effect of initialization? Typically when we learn the decoder variance, it is initialized as 1. However, what if we use other initializations, for example, initialize it at the convergence value, but still set it to be learnable? Maybe it is interesting to study different initializations using the dynamics in figure 4b.

2. I have a question regarding your statement that the using learnable gamma is important for improving sample quality. However, in my understanding, since learnable gamma will result in very small value of gamma, in the end the VAE model is still somewhat under regularized, and directly sample from it should not lead to good sample quality. Previously this is resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but I think the latent model is something else, and strictly speaking the sample obtained by two-stage models are not samples from the VAE. Maybe the authors can discuss a bit on this.

3. Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominanted by the reconstruction loss, and you show that learning gamma and fixing gamma at a small value leads to similar recon loss, do you observe similar performance of OOD detection when gamma is fixed at a small value? Intuitively they should behave similar here.


After rebuttal:
Thanks for addressing my concern, I will keep my score.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the infinite gradient of training VAEs, namely when the decoder's variance is a learnable parameter, the variance will converge to zero, resulting in unbounded gradient. Although previously this is seen as an undesirable property, the authors prove that unbounded gradients are necessary for obtaining optimal spare representations. The authors also try to argue that learning the variance is better than fixing it to an arbitrarily small value.

Main Review:
The paper address an important issue in VAE training. It is widely observe that when the decoder variance is learnable, it will converge to a small number near zero. This paper make a nice connection between this phenomenon and optimal sparse representations, which is arguably the goal for learning an AE or VAE. By proving that it is inevitable to avoid the unbounded gradient while minimizing a generalization of AE loss, the adaptive decoder variance is justified.

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. When you study the learning of decoder variance, do you consider the effect of initialization? Typically when we learn the decoder variance, it is initialized as 1. However, what if we use other initializations, for example, initialize it at the convergence value, but still set it to be learnable? Maybe it is interesting to study different initializations using the dynamics in figure 4b.

2. I have a question regarding your statement that the using learnable gamma is important for improving sample quality. However, in my understanding, since learnable gamma will result in very small value of gamma, in the end the VAE model is still somewhat under regularized, and directly sample from it should not lead to good sample quality. Previously this is resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but I think the latent model is something else, and strictly speaking the sample obtained by two-stage models are not samples from the VAE. Maybe the authors can discuss a bit on this.

3. Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominanted by the reconstruction loss, and you show that learning gamma and fixing gamma at a small value leads to similar recon loss, do you observe similar performance of OOD detection when gamma is fixed at a small value? Intuitively they should behave similar here.


After rebuttal:
Thanks for addressing my concern, I will keep my score.

Yes.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This submission investigates the behavior of the infinite gradient encountered during the training of Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), specifically when the decoder's variance is a parameter learned by the model. The paper highlights that this learnable variance tends to converge towards zero, leading to an issue of unbounded gradients. Contrary to prior perceptions of this as an undesirable characteristic, the authors present a compelling argument, backed by proof, that these unbounded gradients are, in fact, essential for achieving optimal sparse representations. Furthermore, the work posits that allowing the variance to be learned is a superior approach compared to arbitrarily setting it to a small, fixed value.

**Main Review:**
The paper addresses a significant and frequently observed challenge within VAE training: the tendency for the decoder variance to collapse to near-zero values when it is a learnable parameter. A key strength of this work lies in its insightful establishment of a connection between this phenomenon and the pursuit of optimal sparse representations, a recognized objective in the domain of autoencoders and VAEs. The authors effectively justify the adoption of adaptive decoder variance by demonstrating that the occurrence of unbounded gradients is an unavoidable consequence when minimizing a generalized Autoencoder loss function.

I do have a few points that warrant further clarification or discussion:

1.  **Impact of Initialization on Decoder Variance Learning:** When examining the dynamics of learned decoder variance, did the authors consider the influence of different initialization strategies? Standard practice often involves initializing this variance at 1. However, exploring alternative initializations—for instance, setting it to a value close to its eventual convergence point, while still allowing it to be learned—could yield interesting insights. Investigating how such varied initializations affect the dynamics, perhaps building upon the analysis presented in Figure 4b, would be a valuable extension.

2.  **Learnable Gamma and Sample Quality:** The paper makes a claim regarding the importance of a learnable gamma (decoder variance) for enhancing sample quality. However, given that a learnable gamma typically drives the variance to very small values, my understanding suggests that the resulting VAE model might become somewhat under-regularized. This condition usually implies that direct sampling from such a VAE might not yield high-quality samples. While previous research has tackled this by training a secondary generative model on the aggregated posterior, it's crucial to acknowledge that the latent model in such two-stage approaches operates differently, and samples generated this way are not strictly "samples from the VAE" itself. A more in-depth discussion on this aspect, clarifying the authors' definition of "improved sample quality" in this context, would be beneficial.

3.  **OOD Detection Performance with Fixed Small Gamma:** In the context of Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection experiments, it's well-known that the VAE's log-likelihood (or Bits Per Dimension, BPD) is predominantly influenced by the reconstruction loss component. Given the paper's finding that both learning gamma and fixing it at a small value result in comparable reconstruction losses, did the authors observe similar OOD detection performance when gamma was held constant at a small value? Intuitively, one might expect their behavior to be quite similar in this specific task.

**After Rebuttal:**
My concerns have been adequately addressed by the authors' response, and I will maintain my original assessment of the submission.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
Yes, the authors have appropriately discussed these aspects.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary:
**Update after the authors' response**: I appreciate the detailed authors' response, and while it clarifies some points the paper remains a borderline case to me unless the applicability of the findings in the paper was clearly acknowledged and the breadth of the main claims was reduced accordingly. 

**Update after further engagement with authors**: We have managed to clear up some misunderstanding / misinterpretation from both sides, and I have raised my score accordingly from a 5 to a 6.

The paper investigates Autoencoders with a Gaussian likelihood model, where the variance is a trainable parameter. As has been observed before, this often leads to learned variances approaching zero (particularly for high-capacity models and noise-free data), which in turn leads to exploding gradients. The paper argues that these gradients and the collapse of the variance towards very low values should be embraced, rather than being seen as a pathology of the model. In particular the paper argues that under Gaussian likelihood and a fixed latent-space prior, if the goal is to learn a lossless representation of the dataset (which implies a high-fidelity generative model) near unbounded gradients are necessary to avoid underfitting (i.e. using unnecessary latent dimensions). Finally the paper proposes a scheme to mitigate optimization issues with unbounded gradients and illustrates the scheme on CelebA

**Contributions:**

1) Analyzing the origin of unbounded gradients of Gaussian likelihood (V)AEs with learnable (co-)variance. Significance: unsurprisingly, the source of the problem is the Gaussian likelihood term, which is unbounded for the variance approaching zero. The latter has been pointed out various times in the VAE literature and is a well known pathology when fitting Gaussian (mixture) models. 

2) A definition of optimal sparse representations as allowing for zero reconstruction error while only using a minimally required amount of dimensions (lossless compression). Significance: I think the definition can be useful for some particular applications (most notably training a high fidelity generative model), but in practice I would argue there are many clear cases where zero reconstruction error is clearly undesirable for an optimal representation (because the data contains noise or other task-irrelevant variations that should not be captured by the representation). This is well discussed in the literature on lossy compression and representation learning (rate distortion theory and information bottleneck literature and their modern application to neural networks and auto-encoder-like architectures).

3) Showing that, under said definition, “optimal sparse representations” are only attainable via near-unbounded gradients for any AE type architecture. Significance: I think this claim is at best overstated, at worst wrong: since Gaussian likelihoods are the root of the gradient explosion, the findings do not apply for other likelihood models that do not suffer from the pathology of unboundedenss (under a certain parametrization), such as e.g. Bernoulli likelihoods. The claim thus only applies to Gaussian likelihoods and other likelihood models with unbounded values, but not any AE architecture. Please correct me if my understanding (on the reliability of the findings on Gaussian likelihoods) is wrong.
reviews.main_review:
**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper follows up on a previously pointed out pathology, and shows that this pathology is unavoidable and rather than working around it (by limiting gradient magnitude), it should be incorporated into the optimization process (leading to a variance-smoothing scheme). The technical part of the paper is well written, but important conceptual assumptions are not discussed (many works would disagree that optimal representations are lossless), and some claims are made w.r.t. arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Guassian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distribution (i.e. no aggregate posterior). Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met. The experiments shown are insightful, but since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, it remains somewhat unclear how well the  corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements - particularly since comparisons to other methods are missing and the sparsity of the solution obtained is not computed.

**Verdict:**

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct, but the findings are not very surprising, given that the likelihood model is Gaussian. Some of the claims (no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising, but only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well behaved likelihood models - this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions) is too broad - it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g. for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. I am therefore leaning towards rejection, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
 * Thorough analysis of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions the main problem cannot be avoided
 * Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process.
 * Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations)

**Cons:**
 * Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (which, admittedly, are standard modelling assumptions in large parts of the VAE literature).
 * Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
 * Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up, and the single experiment that was run does not have multiple repetitions such that the stat. significance of the empirical findings remains unclear (though I do expect most findings to be significant, the margins look fairly good).

**Improvements:**

1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Main Review:
**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper follows up on a previously pointed out pathology, and shows that this pathology is unavoidable and rather than working around it (by limiting gradient magnitude), it should be incorporated into the optimization process (leading to a variance-smoothing scheme). The technical part of the paper is well written, but important conceptual assumptions are not discussed (many works would disagree that optimal representations are lossless), and some claims are made w.r.t. arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Guassian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distribution (i.e. no aggregate posterior). Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met. The experiments shown are insightful, but since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, it remains somewhat unclear how well the  corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements - particularly since comparisons to other methods are missing and the sparsity of the solution obtained is not computed.

**Verdict:**

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct, but the findings are not very surprising, given that the likelihood model is Gaussian. Some of the claims (no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising, but only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well behaved likelihood models - this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions) is too broad - it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g. for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. I am therefore leaning towards rejection, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
 * Thorough analysis of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions the main problem cannot be avoided
 * Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process.
 * Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations)

**Cons:**
 * Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (which, admittedly, are standard modelling assumptions in large parts of the VAE literature).
 * Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
 * Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up, and the single experiment that was run does not have multiple repetitions such that the stat. significance of the empirical findings remains unclear (though I do expect most findings to be significant, the margins look fairly good).

**Improvements:**

1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
After careful consideration, and following constructive engagement with the authors, I have updated my assessment of this manuscript. While the authors' detailed responses helped clarify several aspects, my initial concerns regarding the precise applicability of the findings and the broadness of certain claims persist to some extent. Nevertheless, through our further discussions, we successfully resolved some mutual misunderstandings, leading me to increase my score from a 5 to a 6.

The paper focuses on Autoencoders that employ a Gaussian likelihood model with a trainable variance parameter. It addresses a well-known phenomenon where, particularly with high-capacity models and noise-free data, learned variances tend to approach zero, leading to exploding gradients. Contrary to viewing this as a model pathology, the authors argue that these gradients and the collapse of variance should be embraced. Their central thesis is that, under a Gaussian likelihood and a fixed latent-space prior, achieving a lossless representation (implying a high-fidelity generative model) necessitates near-unbounded gradients to prevent underfitting by utilizing excessive latent dimensions. The paper concludes by proposing an optimization strategy to mitigate issues arising from these unbounded gradients, demonstrated on the CelebA dataset.

### Contributions:

1.  **Analysis of Unbounded Gradients:** The paper delves into the origin of unbounded gradients in Gaussian likelihood (V)AEs with learnable (co-)variance. However, the significance of this analysis is somewhat diminished given that the unbounded nature of the Gaussian likelihood term as variance approaches zero is a widely recognized issue in the VAE literature and a common pathology in Gaussian model fitting.
2.  **Definition of Optimal Sparse Representations:** The authors propose a definition of optimal sparse representations characterized by zero reconstruction error and minimal dimensionality (i.e., lossless compression). While this definition might be beneficial for specific use cases, such as developing high-fidelity generative models, its general applicability is questionable. In many practical scenarios, an optimal representation deliberately involves lossy compression, as achieving zero reconstruction error can lead to capturing noise or irrelevant variations present in the data. This distinction is extensively explored in the literature on lossy compression, representation learning, rate-distortion theory, and information bottleneck methods, including their contemporary applications in neural network architectures like autoencoders.
3.  **Claim on Optimal Sparse Representations and Unbounded Gradients:** The paper asserts that, under their proposed definition, "optimal sparse representations" necessitate nearly unbounded gradients across *any* autoencoder-type architecture. This assertion appears to be overly broad or potentially inaccurate. The phenomenon of exploding gradients originates from the Gaussian likelihood itself; therefore, these findings would not extend to alternative likelihood models—such as Bernoulli likelihoods—that do not exhibit unbounded behavior under certain parameterizations. Consequently, this claim’s scope is limited to Gaussian likelihoods and other models with unbounded values, rather than encompassing all AE architectures.

### Main Review:

**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**
The paper builds upon an identified pathological behavior in VAEs, arguing for its inevitability and proposing its integration into optimization rather than circumvention, via a variance-smoothing mechanism. The technical exposition is strong. However, several critical conceptual underpinnings are not adequately explored, particularly the assumption that optimal representations must be lossless—a stance many established works would contest. Furthermore, certain claims are presented as applicable to general AE models, yet their validity, to my understanding, is strictly confined to Gaussian likelihood models (and similar likelihoods exhibiting unbounded growth when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distributions (i.e., without considering an aggregate posterior). If these specific assumptions are relaxed, the claims' applicability is diminished. While the presented experiments offer valuable insights, the evaluation of the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is based on a single experiment, which limits the clarity regarding the generalizability of its results and the extent of claimed improvements, especially given the absence of comparative analyses with existing methods and the lack of quantitative assessment of solution sparsity.

**Verdict:**
My overall assessment of this manuscript is that it sits just above the acceptance threshold, following a productive exchange with the authors that clarified some initial misunderstandings. While the paper's analysis appears technically sound, its conclusions are not entirely novel when contextualized within the known behavior of Gaussian likelihood models. Certain assertions, such as the necessity of near-unbounded gradients for optimal representation in *any* AE, initially strike as bold, primarily because the specific assumptions underpinning these claims are not explicitly delineated. My primary reservations, detailed further below, revolve around two key points: first, the theoretical analysis and empirical findings seem to be limited to specific, often ill-behaved, likelihood models, a crucial scope limitation that is not adequately articulated within the paper. Second, the proposed definition of "optimal representations" as strictly lossless compression is overly expansive; while suitable for some applications, it is demonstrably unsuitable for others (e.g., scenarios involving noisy data or where generalization benefits from lossy compression). A comprehensive discussion addressing these nuances is essential. Although these significant concerns permeate various sections of the paper, I believe they are addressable through a diligent revision.

**Pros:**
*   Provides a detailed examination of the core issue, highlighting its inevitability under specific modeling assumptions.
*   Introduces a method to integrate this inherent problem into a more numerically robust optimization framework.
*   Offers a clear and specific definition for the target of optimal sparse lossless representations.

**Cons:**
*   The paper presents claims in a highly generalized manner, despite their reliance on quite specific underlying assumptions (though these are prevalent in much of the VAE literature).
*   The central pathology discussed stems from unbounded likelihoods, a phenomenon previously identified as problematic; the paper's engagement with this existing body of work is somewhat limited.
*   While the experimental results for the variance-smoothing technique appear promising, the supporting evidence is rather sparse, consisting of a single experiment without multiple repetitions, which obscures the statistical significance of the empirical observations (though the reported margins suggest most findings are likely significant).

### Improvements:

1.  I contend that Definition 1, in its current broad formulation, is problematic. For numerous applications, optimal sparse representations are inherently achieved through **lossy** compression, leading to a non-zero reconstruction error. This applies to tasks such as learning robust representations, achieving disentanglement, or performing outlier detection with noisy datasets—essentially any scenario where data is presumed to contain noise or task-irrelevant variations that should ideally be filtered out via compression. A substantial body of literature addresses the nuances of effective representation, including seminal works like "The Information Bottleneck Method" by Tishby et al. (2000), "Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations" by Achille et al. (2018), "Fixing a Broken ELBO" by Alemi et al. (2018), and "Deep Variational Info Bottleneck" by Alemi et al. (2017). The manuscript should dedicate at least one paragraph to discussing this critical distinction, and the definition itself ought to be refined to something akin to "optimal lossless representations for noise-free data" or a similar, more precisely scoped phrasing.
    1a. It is crucial to explicitly delineate the specific contexts where lossless compression is advantageous versus those where it is not. Section 2.2 currently presents a one-sided perspective that needs balancing.
    1b. The discussion of outlier detection with a low \gamma value, as illustrated in Figure 1, relies on the strong assumption of nearly noise-free data residing on a perfectly modeled smooth manifold (implying flawless interpolation and extrapolation). Such conditions are seldom met in real-world applications, necessitating a more nuanced and realistic discussion of these underlying assumptions.
2.  It is imperative that the paper consistently clarifies that its primary claims are exclusively pertinent to models employing Gaussian likelihoods (with trainable variance, which is already adequately specified). Presently, some assertions convey a broader applicability to any AE architecture with arbitrary likelihood functions. This clarification is not intended to diminish the paper's importance—indeed, its focus on a widely used VAE model ensures high relevance—but rather to enhance the precision and scope of its claims and findings. Furthermore, the manuscript would benefit from including a broader message encouraging the community to critically evaluate standard modeling assumptions and explore alternative likelihood models when suitable, a perspective currently absent.
3.  While a zero reconstruction error is indeed a desirable outcome for tasks aiming to develop high-fidelity generative models (e.g., for images) or for density modeling of noise-free data, I would challenge the suitability of variational autoencoders for these specific applications, despite their historical use. It seems evident that employing a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is an inappropriate modeling choice in such contexts. I would even suggest that a fixed prior distribution is often suboptimal, especially when underfitting becomes a concern. Therefore, a discussion of alternative modeling approaches—such as flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, or using "aggregate posteriors" as priors—is strongly recommended. Incorporating these alternatives would significantly benefit readers by providing valuable context and broadening the paper's perspective.
4.  Sparsity, as outlined in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105), is conceptualized as driving the posterior distribution towards the prior for dimensions deemed non-informative. Prior research, particularly within the information bottleneck literature and works like "Fixing a Broken ELBO," has extensively demonstrated that this form of sparsity can be achieved through well-designed lossy compression schemes grounded in rate-distortion theory. A critical aspect of these approaches is that the prior is not fixed but rather derived from the marginal or "aggregate posterior." This mechanism is known to effectively encourage the non-utilization of unnecessary dimensions, as their distribution converges to that of the aggregate posterior. Consequently, I find the statement in Line 327—"Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal"—to be inaccurate. Sparsity can indeed be attained by leveraging an aggregate posterior, as exemplified by methods from Tomzcak and Welling (2018) and the "Deep Variational Info Bottleneck," which employs a variational approximation to the marginal. This point requires discussion and clarification.

### Comments:

*   **Line 345:** I only partially concur with the assertion in Line 345. While adaptable or trainable priors can indeed contribute to achieving sparse representations, it is important to note that if these models still utilize Gaussian likelihoods, they will not inherently circumvent the issue of infinite gradients (a point further elaborated in Improvement 4).
*   **Lines 219-220:** In light of my preceding comments (particularly those detailed in the Improvements section), the statement in Lines 219-220 requires either moderation or explicit clarification regarding the specific assumptions under which it remains valid. These assumptions include Gaussian likelihoods, fixed priors, and adherence to the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as given in Definition 1, distinguishing it from other forms, such as lossy optimal representations.

### Limitations And Societal Impact:

The authors should incorporate a dedicated paragraph that thoroughly discusses the limitations of the proposed methodology, potentially also re-emphasizing the circumscribed scope of the theoretical analysis and the claims derived from it.

### Rating:

**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**
