reviews.summary: The work proposes RotoGrad, a multitask layer that rotates a shared feature space in a deep neural network to align gradients and mitigate negative transfer effects. The proposed method works within models with multiple prediction heads and a shared backbone, and deals both with magnitude and directional imbalance between task gradients. Authors provide multiple experiments in the computer vision space to show efficacy of the proposed method against other multitask baselines.
reviews.main_review:
**Overall:** The paper presents an interesting new method to treat multitask learning systems. If robust, this method can have general use to the community and be a powerful addition to the toolbox. However, there are many unanswered questions and I remained overall unconvinced that the method works without major additional experimentation, including detailed ablation studies, and justifications (too many to address within the short rebuttal period in my opinion). I do think the method shows promise though, so regardless of the decision after this review process I hope the authors continue to work on their method.  

**Pros:**

(P1) Method is clear and intuitive, and tackles a crucial problem within optimal multitask training. 

(P2) Results seem promising and beat other baselines on benchmarks within the main paper. 

(P3) Formulation of the method as a Stackelberg game is interesting and can perhaps provide general insight into these problems, as many modern MTL methods involve optimizing auxiliary losses.

(P4) Results are given for multiple model structures and multiple datasets performing the same tasks. 

(P5) Code is provided for reproducibility. 

**Needs Comment/Revision (major):**

(RM1) Why must the proposed method interact with the forward pass? There are some other methods (see for example Sinha et al. at https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.08028) that propose methods which only interact with the backwards pass and seem to also work well. Either theoretical justification or additional experiments are needed to justify why doing the aggressive thing of altering the forward pass feature space is necessary. 

(RM2) The toy example provided is unconvincing, as the only comparison is to vanilla SGD. It is rather puzzling that even the Rotograd layer within the toy example is optimized using RAdam, which I feel would give Rotograd a severe advantage. 

(RM3) When the proposed method outperforms other methods that’s great, but when all the other methods perform very poorly it raises questions of whether the other methods are implemented correctly. For instance, why is MGDA performing so poorly on the CIFAR/MGDA tasks when the original MGDA paper used that as a benchmark and actually performed quite well? These discrepancies with the prior results need to be addressed for the current results to be fully believed.

(RM4) One strange detail is that GradNorm and GradDrop seem to have different compute overheads within the current implementations, at least within the CelebA experiments. However, as far as I can tell, GradNorm and GradDrop have essentially the same compute overhead (with the limiting factor in the compute overhead the same computation of dL_i/dw for each task loss L_i), so this discrepancy makes me concerned that the present implementations of baselines is not correct. Similarly, RotoGrad seems to be even slower than PCGrad, and PCGrad has to calculate dL_i/dw for all trainable weights while RotoGrad only needs to backpropagate to the feature layer, so this overhead is somewhat concerning as well.

(RM5) Ablation studies are needed to see whether both the magnitude and directional components of Rotograd are both needed. 

(RM6) The magnitude part of the implementation seems to include the initial gradient magnitude as part of the normalized quantity. However, this is incredibly dependent on the network initialization. Did authors account for this in some way? 

(RM7) The digit classification task performances seem to be very dependent on the size of the RotoGrad layer. Looking at the appendix, the network structure for these tasks is very shallow, so how can the authors rule out the effect of the added network capacity within the RotoGrad layer?

**Needs Comment/Revision (less major):**

(RLM1) Proposition 3.1 is correct but is rather obvious - would authors consider rephrasing into nonmathematical language to reduce potential confusion? If two gradients have positive pairwise similarity, then following their sum (for small enough learning rate) will clearly reduce both losses because loss reduction is step_size*(grad_k dot grad_sum). This proposition has also been heavily treated in prior work as well like MGDA and PCGrad, so does not necessarily need independent treatment here and can just be stated.

(RLM2) Authors claim that homogenizing gradients at one layer is equivalent to homogenizing gradients at the weight layers. This is a false statement, as chained gradient computations will cause even initially homogenized gradients at a feature layer to increasingly stray from each other. Authors must justify why they can ignore this effect.

(RLM3) Figures need to be placed closer to the text where they’re referenced. 

(RLM4) Sometimes bolded numbers in tables are not the best results for that column. 

*Post rebuttal*: I thank the authors for their time and effort in crafting their response. A lot of my more technical comments were addressed. I am still very concerned about the extra capacity and don't feel these concerns are properly addressed by pointing out that one other baseline (IMTL-G) also has added capacity, and some of the new results actually may run counter to the proposed narrative - for example RotoGradNorm beating out RotoGrad. The method itself is well defined but I am still not sold on the scientific validity of the analysis. I will keep my rating a 4.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: More discussion could be provided regarding limitations and societal impact. In terms of the former, for example, there are plenty of benchmarks on which RotoGrad seems to not perform the best, so discussion of why needs to be more in detail.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

Main Review:

However, as far as I can tell, GradNorm and GradDrop have essentially the same compute overhead (with the limiting factor in the compute overhead the same computation of dL_i/dw for each task loss L_i), so this discrepancy makes me concerned that the present implementations of baselines is not correct. (RLM1) Proposition 3.1 is correct but is rather obvious - would authors consider rephrasing into nonmathematical language to reduce potential confusion? This proposition has also been heavily treated in prior work as well like MGDA and PCGrad, so does not necessarily need independent treatment here and can just be stated.

(RLM2) Authors claim that homogenizing gradients at one layer is equivalent to homogenizing gradients at the weight layers. This is a false statement, as chained gradient computations will cause even initially homogenized gradients at a feature layer to increasingly stray from each other. (RLM4) Sometimes bolded numbers in tables are not the best results for that column.

I am still very concerned about the extra capacity and don't feel these concerns are properly addressed by pointing out that one other baseline (IMTL-G) also has added capacity, and some of the new results actually may run counter to the proposed narrative - for example RotoGradNorm beating out RotoGrad. The method itself is well defined but I am still not sold on the scientific validity of the analysis. I will keep my rating a 4.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
More discussion could be provided regarding limitations and societal impact. In terms of the former, for example, there are plenty of benchmarks on which RotoGrad seems to not perform the best, so discussion of why needs to be more in detail.

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces RotoGrad, a novel multitask layer designed to enhance deep neural network training. Its core mechanism involves rotating a shared feature space to align gradients, thereby mitigating negative transfer effects, particularly in architectures featuring multiple prediction heads connected to a common backbone. RotoGrad specifically addresses both the magnitude and directional imbalances often observed in task gradients. The authors present various experiments within the domain of computer vision to illustrate the proposed method's efficacy when compared against existing multitask learning baselines.

**Main Review:**
**Overall Assessment:** The RotoGrad method, as presented, offers an intriguing new approach to managing multitask learning systems. Should its robustness be thoroughly substantiated, this technique possesses significant potential for widespread adoption within the research community, serving as a powerful enhancement to the existing methodological toolkit. However, the current submission leaves a considerable number of critical questions unanswered, and I remain largely unconvinced of the method's overall effectiveness without substantial additional experimentation, including comprehensive ablation studies and more rigorous justifications. In my estimation, addressing these numerous issues would likely exceed the scope of a typical rebuttal period. Nevertheless, I do believe the underlying concept holds considerable promise, and I sincerely hope the authors continue to develop and refine their method, irrespective of the outcome of this particular review cycle.

**Strengths:**

(P1) The proposed methodology is presented with commendable clarity and offers an intuitive solution to a critical challenge in achieving optimal multitask training.
(P2) The experimental results reported in the main paper appear promising, demonstrating superior performance against several established baselines on benchmark datasets.
(P3) The formalization of the method using a Stackelberg game framework is an interesting aspect that could potentially yield broader theoretical insights into modern multitask learning approaches, many of which involve optimizing auxiliary loss functions.
(P4) The experimental validation includes multiple model architectures and diverse datasets, all addressing similar tasks, which strengthens the claims of generalizability.
(P5) The availability of code contributes positively to the reproducibility of the reported findings.

**Key Areas Requiring Revision/Comment (Major):**

(RM1) A significant concern pertains to the necessity for the proposed method to intervene in the forward pass. Other methods, such as those detailed by Sinha et al. (e.g., at https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.08028), achieve favorable results by exclusively manipulating the backward pass. The authors must provide either robust theoretical justification or additional empirical evidence to explain why the more aggressive approach of altering the feature space during the forward pass is indispensable.
(RM2) The provided toy example is not entirely convincing. Its only comparative baseline is vanilla SGD, which is a relatively weak point of comparison. Furthermore, it is perplexing that the RotoGrad layer within this toy example is optimized using RAdam, which likely confers a substantial advantage to RotoGrad, making the comparison uneven.
(RM3) While RotoGrad's outperformance of other methods is commendable, the exceptionally poor performance of several baselines raises doubts about the correctness of their implementations. For instance, MGDA performs surprisingly poorly on CIFAR/MGDA tasks, despite the original MGDA paper demonstrating strong results on similar benchmarks. These discrepancies with prior published results must be addressed for the current comparative findings to be fully credible.
(RM4) There are inconsistencies in the reported computational overheads. For example, GradNorm and GradDrop appear to have different compute overheads in the CelebA experiments, which is unexpected given that their primary computational bottleneck (calculating dL_i/dw for each task loss L_i) should lead to similar costs. Similarly, RotoGrad seems to be slower than PCGrad, even though PCGrad requires backpropagating dL_i/dw for all trainable weights, while RotoGrad only needs to backpropagate to the feature layer. These overhead discrepancies are concerning and warrant clarification.
(RM5) Comprehensive ablation studies are essential to ascertain whether both the magnitude-balancing and directional-alignment components of RotoGrad are individually necessary for its observed performance.
(RM6) The implementation of the magnitude component appears to include the initial gradient magnitude as part of the normalized quantity. This approach is highly dependent on the network's initialization. The authors should clarify how they have accounted for or mitigated this sensitivity.
(RM7) Performance on the digit classification tasks seems overly sensitive to the size of the RotoGrad layer. Given the shallow network structure described in the appendix for these tasks, the authors must convincingly rule out the possibility that the observed improvements are simply due to the added network capacity introduced by the RotoGrad layer itself.

**Areas Requiring Revision/Comment (Less Major):**

(RLM1) Proposition 3.1, while technically accurate, is rather self-evident. The authors might consider rephrasing it in more accessible, non-mathematical language to reduce potential confusion. The concept that positive pairwise gradient similarity leads to loss reduction when following their sum (with a sufficiently small learning rate) has been thoroughly explored in prior works like MGDA and PCGrad, and thus might not require independent formal treatment here; a direct statement or reference could suffice.
(RLM2) The claim that homogenizing gradients at a single layer is equivalent to homogenizing them at subsequent weight layers is factually incorrect. Due to the nature of chained gradient computations, even initially aligned gradients at a feature layer will tend to diverge as they propagate through successive weight layers. The authors must provide a robust justification for why this cumulative effect can be disregarded.
(RLM3) For improved readability and enhanced flow, figures should be placed in closer proximity to the corresponding text references within the manuscript.
(RLM4) In some tables, the bolded numbers, intended to highlight the best results, do not consistently represent the optimal performance for that particular column, which can be misleading.

**Post-Rebuttal Commentary:**
I am grateful to the authors for their diligent effort in crafting their response. While many of my more technical comments were addressed, my significant concerns regarding the impact of additional capacity remain largely unresolved. Merely pointing out that another baseline (IMTL-G) also incorporates added capacity does not fully alleviate these fundamental concerns. Furthermore, some of the new results presented in the rebuttal, such as RotoGradNorm outperforming RotoGrad, appear to contradict the primary narrative or expected behavior of the proposed method. While the method itself is clearly defined, I continue to harbor doubts about the robust scientific validation of its underlying analysis. Consequently, I will maintain my initial rating of 4.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The paper would benefit from a more extensive discussion regarding its limitations and potential societal implications. Concerning the former, for example, there are numerous benchmarks where RotoGrad does not achieve the best performance, and a more detailed explanation of why it might underperform in certain scenarios is warranted.

**Rating:**
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: CobBO: Coordinate Backoff Bayesian Optimization with Two-Stage Kernels

reviews.summary: The authors have proposed a new algorithm for black box optimization called CobBO. At a fundamental level, the algorithm is the extension of classic block coordinate ascent to black box optimization using techniques from Bayesian Optimization. The algorithm focuses on a small subset of coordinates in every iteration. In the first step, it uses a simple kernel to interpolate the values of the function learnt at other points to the points in the subspace. In the next step, it uses a full fledged BO with a more sophisticated kernel in the subset to learn the function in this subspace. It balances exploration and exploitation between subspaces by using a backoff stopping rule. The authors have supported their case by extensive empirical studies and some theoretical results.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:
- The paper proposes an interesting algorithm to address the troubles that usually BO based methods face in high dimensional problems. While the basic concept of block coordinate ascent to address high dimensionality is already known and well studied, the authors apply this for BO and take care of several aspects to make it a practically usable algorithm.
- The empirical evaluation of the algorithm seems quite extensive.

Questions for the authors:
- The paper mentions that the scale of RBF is not tuned and is fixed before hand. I am curious as to how this choice affects the results. It is clear that one cannot set an arbitrary value as it might lead  to over smoothing or under smoothing. So is there a range where it works well and how is it chosen? Also how does this choice affect the results?
- While the algorithm is clearly set in the noiseless regime (which is fine), I am curious as to what will happen in the noisy regime? Clearly some of aspects cannot be directly taken over since we would not have access to exact function values. How robust do you expect the algorithm to be to noise in the current version (only allowing for noisy updates of posterior and no additional adaption) ? I am curious because typically one would expect noise in real world scenarios and if the algorithm is sensitive it can break under the noise.
- The description of Sec 3.1 and 3.2 is unclear. There are clarifying details in Appendix C, but overall I could not find one description of the algorithm where the choice of $C_t$ and the backoff is explicitly stated, as used in the experiments. If different versions outlined in Appendix C are used in different experiments, depending on the task, I don't think the comparison is fair since CobBO is allowed to make some changes depending on the function but others are not. I think it would be really helpful and clear if the stopping rule and choice of subspace is exactly mentioned in the main paper.
- Could you elaborate the intuition behind the description of the stopping rule outlined in Appendix C? It seems like too precise a definition without sufficient description as to how the specific form was obtained.
- Is the value $|C_t|$ chosen uniformly at random in $\{1,2, \dots, 30\}$? 
- In the ablation study, when only of one parameters is studied, what is the value of the other ones which are kept constant?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors have proposed a new algorithm for black box optimization called CobBO. At a fundamental level, the algorithm is the extension of classic block coordinate ascent to black box optimization using techniques from Bayesian Optimization. The algorithm focuses on a small subset of coordinates in every iteration. In the first step, it uses a simple kernel to interpolate the values of the function learnt at other points to the points in the subspace. In the next step, it uses a full fledged BO with a more sophisticated kernel in the subset to learn the function in this subspace. It balances exploration and exploitation between subspaces by using a backoff stopping rule. The authors have supported their case by extensive empirical studies and some theoretical results.

Main Review:
Pros:
- The paper proposes an interesting algorithm to address the troubles that usually BO based methods face in high dimensional problems. While the basic concept of block coordinate ascent to address high dimensionality is already known and well studied, the authors apply this for BO and take care of several aspects to make it a practically usable algorithm.
- The empirical evaluation of the algorithm seems quite extensive.

Questions for the authors:
- The paper mentions that the scale of RBF is not tuned and is fixed before hand. I am curious as to how this choice affects the results. It is clear that one cannot set an arbitrary value as it might lead  to over smoothing or under smoothing. So is there a range where it works well and how is it chosen? Also how does this choice affect the results?
- While the algorithm is clearly set in the noiseless regime (which is fine), I am curious as to what will happen in the noisy regime? Clearly some of aspects cannot be directly taken over since we would not have access to exact function values. How robust do you expect the algorithm to be to noise in the current version (only allowing for noisy updates of posterior and no additional adaption) ? I am curious because typically one would expect noise in real world scenarios and if the algorithm is sensitive it can break under the noise.
- The description of Sec 3.1 and 3.2 is unclear. There are clarifying details in Appendix C, but overall I could not find one description of the algorithm where the choice of $C_t$ and the backoff is explicitly stated, as used in the experiments. If different versions outlined in Appendix C are used in different experiments, depending on the task, I don't think the comparison is fair since CobBO is allowed to make some changes depending on the function but others are not. I think it would be really helpful and clear if the stopping rule and choice of subspace is exactly mentioned in the main paper.
- Could you elaborate the intuition behind the description of the stopping rule outlined in Appendix C? It seems like too precise a definition without sufficient description as to how the specific form was obtained.
- Is the value $|C_t|$ chosen uniformly at random in $\{1,2, \dots, 30\}$? 
- In the ablation study, when only of one parameters is studied, what is the value of the other ones which are kept constant?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces CobBO, a novel algorithm designed for black-box optimization. Fundamentally, CobBO extends the classic block coordinate ascent methodology to the black-box domain by integrating techniques from Bayesian Optimization. The algorithm strategically focuses on a small subset of coordinates in each iteration. It employs a two-stage process: first, a simple kernel interpolates function values from observed points to the current subspace; second, a more sophisticated kernel is used within this subspace for a full Bayesian Optimization step. The balance between exploring new subspaces and exploiting promising ones is managed through a specific backoff stopping rule. The authors substantiate their claims with extensive empirical investigations and supporting theoretical derivations.

**Main Review:**

**Strengths:**
*   The proposed algorithm offers an intriguing approach to tackle the well-known challenges that Bayesian Optimization-based methods typically encounter in high-dimensional problem spaces. While the foundational principle of employing block coordinate ascent to manage high dimensionality is a recognized and thoroughly studied technique, the authors skillfully adapt this concept for Bayesian Optimization, meticulously addressing various practical considerations to yield a usable and effective algorithm.
*   The empirical validation of the algorithm's performance is notably comprehensive and robust.

**Points for Clarification and Further Consideration:**

*   **Impact of RBF Scale**: The paper states that the Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel scale is predetermined rather than adaptively tuned. I am keen to understand the sensitivity of the results to this choice. An arbitrary scale could clearly lead to issues like excessive smoothing or undersmoothing. Could the authors elaborate on whether a particular range of values performs optimally and how this range is identified? Furthermore, a discussion on how variations in this fixed scale influence the algorithm's performance would be beneficial.
*   **Performance in Noisy Environments**: The algorithm is presented within a noiseless optimization framework, which is a valid starting point. However, real-world applications frequently involve noisy function evaluations. I am curious about the algorithm's expected behavior and robustness in such noisy regimes. Some current aspects might not directly translate when exact function values are unavailable. How resilient do the authors anticipate the current version of CobBO to be to noise, considering it only accounts for noisy posterior updates without additional noise-specific adaptations? Understanding its sensitivity to noise is crucial, as real-world noise could potentially compromise its effectiveness.
*   **Algorithmic Description Clarity**: Sections 3.1 and 3.2, which describe the algorithm, lack sufficient clarity. While Appendix C provides additional details, there isn't a single, explicit description of the algorithm's exact configuration (specifically, the selection of the subspace $C_t$ and the backoff stopping rule) as it was implemented in the empirical studies. If different versions outlined in Appendix C were employed across various experiments depending on the specific task, this could potentially introduce an unfair comparison, as CobBO might be allowed task-specific modifications that other baseline methods are not. To enhance clarity and reproducibility, it would be highly beneficial to explicitly detail the precise stopping rule and subspace selection mechanism within the main body of the paper.
*   **Intuition for Stopping Rule**: Could the authors provide a more in-depth explanation of the rationale behind the specific formulation of the stopping rule described in Appendix C? Its definition appears quite precise, yet the intuitive reasoning or the process through which this particular form was derived is not sufficiently elaborated.
*   **Subspace Size Determination**: Is the magnitude of the subspace, $|C_t|$, selected uniformly at random from the set $\{1, 2, \dots, 30\}$? Clarification on this selection process would be helpful.
*   **Ablation Study Parameters**: During the ablation studies, when only a single parameter is varied, what were the specific constant values assigned to the other parameters that remained fixed?

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**
The authors have adequately addressed these aspects.

**Overall Assessment and Recommendation:**
This paper introduces a well-conceived and empirically strong algorithm that effectively addresses a significant challenge in high-dimensional black-box optimization. The proposed extension of block coordinate ascent to Bayesian Optimization is innovative and demonstrates practical utility. While the points raised above primarily concern clarifications and explorations of robustness, they do not detract from the fundamental soundness or contribution of the work. The extensive empirical validation further strengthens the paper's claims.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept.

reviews.summary: This paper aims at making Bayesian optimization scalable to problems with large dimensional inputs. The authors propose an approach that selects block coordinates of the input space to explore at each iteration. The authors provide some theoretical results to support the proposed approach, and report experimental results to demonstrate that the proposed approach can achieve better performance than existing approaches.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: 
The main idea of the proposed approach is to use the multiplicative weights update method in Bayesian optimization. To my limited knowledge about Bayesian optimization, this is novel and thus the originality is reasonable. 


Quality:
While the approach seems reasonable and the experimental results look promising, I have the following concerns about the paper. 

- The way they cite some of the existing works is not very precise, and thus I don't have a good impression of the quality of the work. For instance, [6] and [7] are the same book;  [26] on inverse distance weighting is Wikipedia; [32] and [33] are the same paper; [40] on Matern kernels is a webpage of scikit learn; [50] on radial basis functions is a webpage of scipy; [61] and [62] are the same paper; [64] and [65] are the same paper. 

- In lines 32-34, the authors say ``always exactly using the values on the queried points as the conditional events may mismatch the function’s local landscape by overemphasizing the approximation accuracy of the global landscape.'' Similar arguments are made in lines 43-48. I feel confused to see this argument. In GP regression, we usually assume noisy outputs, and the use of a regularized matrix inversion (where the regularizer is given by the noise variance) incorporates this assumption by automatically smoothing the outputs. 

- In line 57, the authors say ``CobBO uses the Automatic Relevance Determination (ARD) Matérn 5/2 kernel.'' But it is not clear how the authors specify the weights of this ARD kernel, as this is also not described in the supplementary. And the authors do not explain the value of the length scale parameter in the kernel. 

- In line 12 of Algorithm 1, there exists an RBF interpolation, denoted by $R$. The authors should describe the explicit from this interpolator. While the authors seem to explain that the RBF interpolation and GP regression are different, they are essentially the same approach. As such, RBF interpolation requires the computational complexity cubic to the data size. This point should also be discussed.

- In line 14 of Algorithm 1, $\Omega_t$ denotes the subspace spanned by the block coordinates $C_t$. I am wondering about how the authors specify the values for the coordinates in $\Omega_t$ not selected in $C_t$. For instance, suppose the dimensions are $D = 4$ and $C_t = \{1, 2\}$. Then is $x \in \Omega_t$ given in the form $x = (x_1, x_2, 0, 0)$ by specifying the coordinates not in $C_t$ (i.e,. \{3, 4\}) to be zeros? This point is not clear from the presentation. If this is the case, the authors implicitly make an assumption about the optimal solution that some of their coordinates are zero. This is rather a strong assumption.

- In Section 3.3, the authors define ``regret'' in terms of the selection of the best coordinate block. This regret is different from the regrets defined in terms of the global optimizer, usually analyzed in the Bayesian optimization literature. Therefore, the theoretical results of the paper do not really provide a theoretical guarantee for the proposed approach in terms of the convergence to the global optimizer.  

- While the authors show the hyperparameter settings in Table 2 of the supplementary, some information is still missing, such as the acquisition function used and the length scales of the kernels in RBF interpolation and GP regression. 


Clarity:
- Since the authors omit the concrete expressions for many mathematical concepts (e.g., RBF interpolation and the subspace $\Omega_t$), it is not very easy to deeply understand the mechanism of the proposed algorithm. Also, some key information for reproducing the experimental results is missing. 

Significance:
- There are many hyperparameters that need to be chosen in the proposed algorithm. (Table 2 in the supplementary shows an incomplete list of the hyperparameters.) If the performance of the proposed algorithm is sensitive to the choice of these parameters, the algorithm is not easy to be used by practitioners. This point is not clear from the paper. If this is the case, the significance of the proposed method is limited. 

- Also, as described above, it may be the case that the proposed approach implicitly assumes that some of the coordinates of the global optimizer are zeros. If this is the case, the proposed approach can only be applied to problems that satisfy this assumption, which may be restrictive.


------------ Post rebuttal --------------
As the authors made some clarifications, I have increased my score to 5. Still, however, I'm not very convinced by the argument of the authors on regret analysis.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors do not discuss the limitations of their work.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This submission introduces a method designed to enhance the scalability of Bayesian optimization when dealing with high-dimensional input spaces. The authors' proposed approach involves dynamically selecting specific block coordinates of the input space for exploration during each iteration. They provide theoretical justifications for their methodology and present empirical results suggesting that their technique outperforms existing alternatives.

**Main Review:**

**Originality:**
The central concept of integrating the multiplicative weights update mechanism into the framework of Bayesian optimization appears to be a novel contribution, based on my current understanding of the field. Consequently, the work demonstrates a commendable level of originality.

**Quality:**
While the proposed methodology appears sound and the experimental outcomes are encouraging, I have several substantial reservations concerning the overall quality of the paper:

*   **Citation Practices:** The precision of citations for some existing works is notably lacking, which detracts from the perceived rigor of the research. For instance, several references are duplicated (e.g., [6] and [7] point to the same book; [32] and [33] refer to the same paper; [61] and [62], as well as [64] and [65], are also identical papers). Furthermore, some citations point to non-academic sources, such as Wikipedia for inverse distance weighting ([26]), or general web pages like scikit-learn ([40]) for Matern kernels and scipy ([50]) for radial basis functions. This pattern raises concerns about the thoroughness of the literature review.

*   **Misleading Argument on GP Regression:** The assertions made in lines 32-34 and reiterated in lines 43-48, suggesting that "always exactly using the values on the queried points as the conditional events may mismatch the function’s local landscape by overemphasizing the approximation accuracy of the global landscape," are perplexing. In standard Gaussian Process (GP) regression, the assumption of noisy outputs is typically handled by incorporating a regularized matrix inversion (where the regularizer often corresponds to noise variance), which inherently smooths the outputs and accounts for local variations without overemphasis.

*   **ARD Kernel Specification:** Line 57 states that "CobBO uses the Automatic Relevance Determination (ARD) Matérn 5/2 kernel." However, the paper (and supplementary material) fails to clarify how the weights for this ARD kernel are determined or specified. Additionally, the value of the length scale parameter within the kernel remains unexplained.

*   **RBF Interpolation Details:** Algorithm 1, line 12, introduces an RBF interpolation denoted by $R$. The authors need to provide the explicit mathematical form of this interpolator. While the paper implies a distinction between RBF interpolation and GP regression, these methods are conceptually very similar, and critically, RBF interpolation also incurs a computational complexity cubic with respect to the data size. This computational aspect should be explicitly discussed.

*   **Ambiguity of Subspace Definition:** In Algorithm 1, line 14, $\Omega_t$ is defined as the subspace spanned by the block coordinates $C_t$. It is unclear how the coordinates *not* selected in $C_t$ are handled when defining a point $x \in \Omega_t$. For example, if the total dimension $D=4$ and $C_t=\{1, 2\}$, does this imply that $x = (x_1, x_2, 0, 0)$ by setting the unselected coordinates (3 and 4) to zero? If this is indeed the case, the authors are implicitly making a strong and potentially restrictive assumption that the optimal solution possesses zero values for certain coordinates. This point requires clarification.

*   **Non-Standard Regret Definition:** The definition of "regret" in Section 3.3 is based on the selection of the best coordinate block, which deviates significantly from the standard definitions of regret in the Bayesian optimization literature, which typically focus on convergence to the global optimizer. Consequently, the theoretical results presented do not offer a direct guarantee regarding the proposed approach's convergence to the true global optimum.

*   **Incomplete Hyperparameter Information:** While Table 2 in the supplementary material lists some hyperparameter settings, crucial information is still missing. Specifically, the acquisition function employed and the length scales of the kernels used in both RBF interpolation and GP regression are not provided, hindering reproducibility.

**Clarity:**
The paper's clarity is hampered by the omission of concrete mathematical expressions for several key concepts, such as the RBF interpolation and the precise definition of the subspace $\Omega_t$. This lack of detail makes it challenging to thoroughly grasp the inner workings of the proposed algorithm. Furthermore, the absence of some critical information needed to reproduce the experimental results significantly impacts the paper's transparency.

**Significance:**
The practical significance of the proposed algorithm is questionable due to several factors:

*   **Hyperparameter Sensitivity:** The algorithm involves numerous hyperparameters (with an incomplete list provided in Table 2 of the supplementary material). The paper does not clarify whether the algorithm's performance is highly sensitive to the specific choices of these parameters. If such sensitivity exists, it would severely limit the method's ease of use and applicability for practitioners.

*   **Restrictive Assumptions:** As highlighted previously, the approach might implicitly assume that certain coordinates of the global optimizer are zero. If this is true, the method's applicability would be confined to problems that satisfy this rather strong assumption, thereby restricting its overall utility and generalizability.

--- Post-rebuttal ---
Following the authors' clarifications, I have adjusted my score upwards to a 5. However, I remain unconvinced by the authors' arguments regarding the regret analysis, which continues to be a significant concern.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors have not included a discussion of the limitations inherent in their work, nor have they addressed any potential societal impacts.

**Rating:**
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new High-dim BO framework inspired by Block coordinate ascent. Although similar, this paper differs from other works which leverages a low dimensional structure assumption in that it utilizes all the dimension in the function space. Aside from the overall scheme, its other contributions include (1) an update scheme for the coordinate sampling distribution; (2) a heuristic stopping rule to balance between exploiting current coordinate block and exploring new coordinate blocks; and (3) a theoretical analysis that upper bounds the total regret on a surrogate loss function. The framework shows some promising results when compared with other high-dim BO works.
reviews.main_review:
--Clarity
The paper is generally well written and easy to follow. I have some minor confusions:

1. In the algorithm, the posterior is described as p(\hat{f}| \hat{H}). Is the posterior specified by a GP with low dimensional kernel or high dimensional kernel, but the length-scales on inactive coordinates are frozen (not learned)?.

2. At the beginning of section 4.2, it was claimed that CobBO was compared to REMBO and ATPE but I don't see the comparison anywhere. Also, what would  BOHB be in Fig.5 ?

--Originality
The motivation behind doing BO along a subset of coordinates has been explored many times in the literature. This paper argues that the proposed method, CobBO, "efficiently leverages all the observations in the whole space ... rather than only relying on limited observations in each coordinate subspace". As far as I understand, DROPOUT [35] also re-selects the active coordinates at every iteration (and fill in the remaining coordinates using some heuristic strategy), which guarantees making use of all coordinates in the long run. However, I believe that the proposed subspace selection algorithm is more informed due to the update scheme of the sampling distribution.
--Quality
My main concern with this paper is that the theoretical analysis is not conventional (with respect to the BO literature). In my experience, BO regret analysis usually measures the regret on the objective function itself rather than a surrogate loss. It could be useful to assert that the sampling scheme will, in the asymptotic sense, always pick the right subspace to improve the objective the most number of times; but it does not inform us by how much does it improve the objective. It seems to me that the proposed regret bound is an analysis of the coordinate descent aspect alone, and is entirely independent of the optimization routine within each selected subspace (i.e., if I replace BO by something else, would anything change?). 

Please refer to the Significance subsection for my comments on the experiments.

--Significance
1. For some experiments (e.g., Levy 200D and Ackley 200D), I don't think all methods have converged by the end of 10000 iterations. It would probably take longer to conclude that one is clearly superior to another. 
2. I would suggest some ablation study to show the effect of having variable coordinate block size |C_t| (as I understand, it is now capped at 30). On average, how many coordinates are sampled per iterations? Is there a lower bound to prevent the scheme from selecting none? How does it affect performance if we increase/decrease the upper bound?
3. It was claimed that REMBO takes too long for Ackley 200D, but I dont see how it is the case if we limit the number of effective dimension (to be roughly the same as the average no. selected coordinates by CobBO)? Seems to me that both REMBO and CobBO optimize a low dimensional GP at every iteration with the same no. observations, so what makes one significantly slower than the other?

Although similar, this paper differs from other works which leverages a low dimensional structure assumption in that it utilizes all the dimension in the function space. Aside from the overall scheme, its other contributions include (1) an update scheme for the coordinate sampling distribution; (2) a heuristic stopping rule to balance between exploiting current coordinate block and exploring new coordinate blocks; and (3) a theoretical analysis that upper bounds the total regret on a surrogate loss function. The framework shows some promising results when compared with other high-dim BO works.

Main Review:

3. reviews.text:
## Rewritten Peer Review

**Summary:**
This submission presents a novel high-dimensional Bayesian Optimization (BO) framework, termed CobBO, which draws inspiration from block coordinate ascent methodologies. A key differentiator from existing work, which often assumes low-dimensional intrinsic structures, is CobBO's ability to engage with all dimensions within the function space. Beyond its overarching design, the paper's notable contributions include (1) an adaptive mechanism for updating the coordinate sampling distribution; (2) a heuristic stopping criterion designed to balance the exploitation of current coordinate blocks with the exploration of new ones; and (3) a theoretical analysis that establishes an upper bound on the total regret, albeit on a surrogate loss function. Preliminary experimental evaluations demonstrate encouraging performance when benchmarked against other high-dimensional BO techniques.

**Main Review:**

**Clarity:**
The manuscript is generally well-structured and straightforward to comprehend. However, I encountered a few minor points that could benefit from clarification:

1.  Regarding the algorithm's description, the posterior is denoted as p(\hat{f}| \hat{H}). It would be helpful to clarify whether this posterior is defined by a Gaussian Process (GP) employing a low-dimensional kernel, or if it uses a high-dimensional kernel where the length-scales corresponding to inactive coordinates are held constant (i.e., not learned).
2.  Section 4.2 begins with a statement claiming comparisons between CobBO and both REMBO and ATPE, yet these specific comparisons do not appear to be presented within the text or figures. Additionally, the identity of "BOHB" in Figure 5 requires explicit labeling.

**Originality:**
The fundamental concept of performing Bayesian Optimization along a subset of coordinates has been extensively explored in prior literature. The authors contend that their proposed method, CobBO, distinguishes itself by "efficiently leveraging all the observations in the whole space ... rather than only relying on limited observations in each coordinate subspace." While this is a compelling claim, it's worth noting that approaches like DROPOUT [35] also iteratively re-select active coordinates and employ heuristic strategies for the remaining dimensions, thereby ensuring comprehensive utilization of all coordinates over time. Nevertheless, I perceive CobBO's subspace selection algorithm as potentially more sophisticated and informed, primarily due to its dynamic update scheme for the sampling distribution.

**Quality:**
My primary reservation concerning this paper lies in the unconventional nature of its theoretical analysis, particularly when viewed within the context of established BO literature. Typically, regret analysis in Bayesian Optimization directly quantifies regret with respect to the true objective function, rather than a surrogate loss. While establishing that the sampling scheme asymptotically selects the most beneficial subspace for improvement is valuable, this surrogate loss bound does not directly inform us about the magnitude of improvement achieved on the actual objective function. It appears that the presented regret bound primarily scrutinizes the coordinate descent facet of the algorithm, seeming somewhat detached from the specific optimization routine (i.e., BO) applied within each selected subspace. This raises a question as to whether the analysis would fundamentally change if Bayesian Optimization were replaced by another optimization strategy within the blocks.

For comments pertaining to the experimental evaluation, please refer to the "Significance" section below.

**Significance:**
My assessment of the experimental results and their implications leads to the following observations and suggestions:

1.  In several experiments, notably Levy 200D and Ackley 200D, it is not clear that all compared methods have fully converged within the allotted 10,000 iterations. To draw definitive conclusions about the superiority of one method over another, longer run times might be necessary.
2.  I recommend including an ablation study to investigate the impact of varying the coordinate block size, |C_t|, which I understand is currently capped at 30. This study should address: (a) the average number of coordinates sampled per iteration; (b) whether a lower bound exists to prevent the selection of zero coordinates; and (c) how performance is affected by increasing or decreasing the upper bound.
3.  The claim that REMBO is significantly slower for Ackley 200D requires further clarification. If the number of effective dimensions in REMBO is constrained to be roughly equivalent to the average number of coordinates selected by CobBO, it's not immediately apparent why REMBO would incur a substantially higher computational cost. Both methods would, in principle, be optimizing a low-dimensional GP with a comparable number of observations per iteration, so the source of this claimed speed disparity needs to be elucidated.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors have provided a reasonable discussion regarding the broader impact of their work. I do not foresee any significant potential negative societal implications stemming from this research.

**Rating:**
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Distributional Generalization: Characterizing Classifiers Beyond Test Error

reviews.summary: The paper empirically characterizes a fine-grained generalization property of interpolating learning algorithms, and give a conjecture that formalizes the observed phenomenon. Roughly, the paper observes that the generalization properties of an interpolating classifier can be quite distinct for different sample subpopulations with the same label, whenever the label noise imposed on these subpopulations during the training phase is distinct. Authors conjecture that the generalization properties may be uniform only for samples belonging to a smaller "distinguishable" clusters of the input data space.
reviews.main_review:
Post-rebuttal: I thank authors for their detailed response. Most of the authors' response made a perfect sense. One thing that prevented me from raising the score further is that I am still not sure about is whether the conjecture itself is meaningful or useful. In fact, after reading the response to my first point (Converse...), I am slightly more pessimistic about the possibility that "distinguishability" and "feature calibration" are tightly connected concepts (say, in a causal sense). I am curious if there are alternate (and perhaps more useful) conjectures that can help explain the phenomenon. In other words, I think the paper should benefit from having an extensive discussion that justifies why the proposed conjecture based on distinguishability is nontrivial, useful, and better than other possible alternatives.


---

__What I like.__ The reported empirical observation is intriguing to some degree, and (in my opinion) is worthy of documenting and formalizing. Authors validate the observation under a variety of scenarios in Section 1 & 4 (and also appendices), which helped convincing me of the existence of such phenomenon. As the authors noted, establishing a theoretical framework that can explain the depicted phenomenon may be an interesting open problem (perhaps we can phrase problems in a minimax style with multiple tasks?). The clarity of the writing is well above average, while there are several points that can be clarified further. I also liked the theoretical contributions, which could help readers grasp the idea of how such conjectures can be proved.

__Checking/refining the conjecture.__ On the one hand, I find that the provided empirical observations support the conjecture well. On the other hand, I think that one can even further strengthen the argument by trying to provide an evidence supporting that the conditions described in the conjecture is indeed essential for the distributional similarity. I am actually very curious whether the "distinguishable features" are really essential. Is there a case where the distributional similarity does not hold for indistinguishable features, which is perhaps more informative than the perfectly random case? Although this is not really necessary if one is interested only in the validity of the conjecture, having such evidence will help us understand how non-vacuous/"tight" the conjecture is.

__Clarifying the terminology: Interpolating.__ I think the term "interpolating classifier" can be clarified further in the text. Although the terminology is now (sort of) popularly used in the literature, I think still they need to be properly defined at least once in the main text, as the modern use of this terminology are often wider than what is used to mean in the classic kernel-based literature.

__Clarifying the terminology: Distributional Closeness.__ I am curious in what sense the notion of "distributional closeness" introduced in Section 2 is different from the widely used notion of integral probability metrics (IPMs). If they are identical, sticking to the existing notation would help reduce the confusion.

__Practical implication.__ While I mostly liked the theoretical implications illustrated in Section 1.3, I wonder if authors could give an idea why capturing such fine-grained generalization properties may be important. For instance, could there be any implications to the fairness or shortcut learning?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitation is clearly described, in my opinion. This work mainly discusses what is implicitly taking place in the standard learning practices, instead of giving a new one. I do not think there is any "potential negative impact" that should be discussed.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper presents an empirical investigation into a nuanced generalization characteristic of interpolating learning algorithms, proposing a conjecture to formalize the observed phenomenon. Specifically, the authors identify that the generalization behavior of an interpolating classifier can vary significantly across different subpopulations of samples sharing the same label, particularly when these subpopulations are subjected to distinct levels of label noise during training. The central conjecture posits that uniform generalization properties might only be observed for samples belonging to more "distinguishable" clusters within the input data space.

**Main Review:**
Following the detailed rebuttal provided by the authors, I appreciate the clarity and coherence of most of their responses. However, a lingering concern that prevents me from recommending a higher score relates to the fundamental utility and significance of the proposed conjecture. My initial reservations about its meaningfulness have, in fact, been somewhat reinforced after considering the authors' reply to my point regarding the "Converse..." aspect. I remain skeptical about a strong, particularly causal, link between "distinguishability" and "feature calibration." I would be interested to see if alternative (and potentially more impactful) conjectures could offer a richer explanation for the observed phenomenon. Therefore, I believe the paper would significantly benefit from a more thorough discussion that articulates the non-trivial nature, practical usefulness, and superiority of their distinguishability-based conjecture compared to other potential theoretical explanations.

**Strengths:**
I find the empirical observations presented in this work to be quite compelling and deserving of formal documentation. The authors have diligently validated these observations across a diverse set of scenarios, as detailed in Sections 1 and 4, along with the appendices, which effectively substantiates the existence of the described phenomenon. As the authors themselves acknowledge, developing a robust theoretical framework to fully explain this phenomenon represents a fascinating open research challenge (perhaps amenable to formulation as a minimax problem involving multiple tasks). The overall clarity of the manuscript's writing is commendable, though a few specific areas could still benefit from further refinement. Furthermore, the theoretical contributions are valuable, offering insights into potential avenues for formally proving such conjectures.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

*   **Refining the Conjecture's Scope:** While the empirical data presented strongly supports the proposed conjecture, I believe the argument could be bolstered by demonstrating the *essentiality* of the conditions outlined in the conjecture for achieving distributional similarity. Specifically, I am particularly interested in whether "distinguishable features" are truly indispensable. Could the authors explore a scenario where distributional similarity fails to hold for *indistinguishable* features, perhaps in a context more structured than a purely random case? While not strictly mandatory for asserting the conjecture's validity, providing such evidence would significantly enhance our understanding of how non-vacuous and "tight" the conjecture truly is.

*   **Terminology Clarification: "Interpolating Classifier":** The term "interpolating classifier" would benefit from a more explicit definition within the main body of the text. Despite its increasing prevalence in contemporary literature, its modern usage often encompasses a broader range of meanings than its traditional application in kernel-based methods, warranting a clear explanation for readers.

*   **Terminology Clarification: "Distributional Closeness":** I am curious about the precise relationship between the concept of "distributional closeness" introduced in Section 2 and established notions such as integral probability metrics (IPMs). If these concepts are indeed equivalent, adopting the standard IPM notation would likely improve clarity and prevent potential confusion among readers.

*   **Practical Significance:** While I largely appreciate the theoretical implications discussed in Section 1.3, I believe the paper would be strengthened by elaborating on *why* understanding these fine-grained generalization properties holds practical importance. For example, could there be discernible implications for areas such as algorithmic fairness or the phenomenon of shortcut learning?

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The paper's limitations are, in my assessment, adequately articulated. This research primarily sheds light on implicit dynamics within standard learning paradigms rather than introducing novel methodologies. I perceive no obvious "potential negative impact" that requires further discussion.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary:
This submission proposes a new type of generalization satisfied by interpolating classifiers (the primary focus), that is different from agreement between test error and train error as in classical learning theory. This type of generalization is largely motivated by empirical evidence across different models (neural networks, kernel regression & svm, decision trees) and theoretically for 1-Nearest Neighbors classification (Theorem 1). More specifically, the submission claims that for certain types of mappings x -> L(x) (termed "distinguishable features" -- i.e. they must be learnable to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration), the joint distribution of (L(x), y) where (x,y) pairs are drawn from the true distribution will be "close" to the joint distribution of (L(x), f(x)) on the test set, where f(x) is the trained model prediction.

List of contributions:
--Proposes that interpolating classifiers satisfy "distributional generalization." This is first more specifically formulated as the "Feature Calibration Conjecture" (FCC) in Section 3. A crucial component of this is to identify the types of features L(x) for which the calibration holds (Definition 1); roughly, if the feature is learnable from inputs x to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration (fixed algorithm, data distribution, number of samples, allowable error), then under that same setting calibration will be achieved. The authors prove FCC is true for 1-Nearest neighbors. The Feature Calibration Conjecture is not fully specified, in that there is an assumption of "natural distribution" D (which is such that the conjecture holds in the empirical settings considered).

--Empirical test of the FCC. The types of distinguishable features L considered include (i) the constant partition (calibration then implies the marginal distribution p(y) of the predicted labels matches that of the true label distribution); (ii) a "coarse partition" (L(x) is an "easy" classification task) and calibration holds for fine-grained label subgroups, for instance in Imagenet; (iii) "class partition" (classes that can be learned well from standard datasets, and the true label distribution is a noisy corruption of this); (iv) multiple features. Models investigated include neural networks (WideResnets and MLPs), kernels (standard and high-performing ones), and decision trees. 

--A definition for "distributional generalization" which subsumes the definition of feature calibration & classical generalization, and very preliminary evidence that this holds for non-interpolating models (a Wide Resnet model on CIFAR-10 during training).
reviews.main_review:
Originality: This work identifies & investigates an original empirical property that appears to be satisfied by interpolating classifiers. The proposed concepts (Feature Calibration) are general & seem distinct from any prior work to the best of my knowledge. (For example, the authors note that "standard" calibration is a particular case of Feature Calibration with specific choice of L(x).) I think this work is bold in what it seeks to accomplish (asking a fundamentally new question at the basis of learning theory, building evidence from diverse empirical examples) and makes a reasonable first step towards doing so. 

Quality: The quality of the work seems high. The authors give a number of different empirical examples with sufficient coverage across model diversity and provide a theoretically satisfied case (1-Nearest Neighbors). The authors note the limitations of the work (e.g. the FCC is specified in terms of "natural distributions" which has an unclear meaning) and are fairly thorough in addressing potential concerns & connections to other works. I was not able to identify significant shortcomings with respect to the conceptual formulation or message of the paper. I make some comments about diversity of empirical examples (with respect to datasets / partitions) below.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and was straightforward to read.

Significance: I think this paper takes a risk in its premise, provides a reasonable amount of evidence in support of its main claims, and would spur further work in this area; as I was also not able to identify any major issues with the paper, this is the reason behind my suggested score. In Section 1.3 the authors discuss a number of consequences for learning theory which seem notable. This work tells us more about the implicit bias of models (though not just neural networks); most interesting I think is that in certain limits (e.g. when models are interpolating, and model size >> dataset size), models are not approaching the Bayes optimal classifier but sample from the distribution p(y|x). 

Other comments & questions:

--I find some mentions of "Bayes optimal classifier" to be confusing at times -- for instance, its usage in L268 seems to be with respect to argmax p(y | L(x)) (rather than argmax p(y|x)), if I understand correctly. Perhaps it is worth pointing this out and the connection between the two (e.g. in the setting where p(y|x) is discussed, section starting at L107). 

--In the empirical examples with label noise, why do the authors consider only sparse confusion matrices?

--I think this work has good empirical diversity across very distinct classes of interpolating models, but one place where the paper could be noticeably strengthened is diversity of examples with respect to the datasets / partitions L(x) that are considered. Apart from the constant partition L(x) = 0, all the partitions seem to be largely based off of semantic distinctions that are already available in datasets. (Because of this, I also wondered whether the behavior is largely inherited from the dataset and from the existing semantic structure within it.) Are there more complex L(x), that are not semantically constructed, that can be considered? Separate but related to this, is it valid to consider L(x) that have some knowledge of the distribution D, or even a particular training set? For instance, L(x) that is constructed from trained intermediate layer embeddings in a neural network -- or would L(x) that has knowledge of the distribution D potentially break FCC?

(For instance, this type of L(x) / y constructed from true labels / label noise on existing label structure, e.g. CIFAR-10, is also used in the non-interpolating setting in Section 5.2, which makes the reader wonder what is actually happening in the interpolating setting. More generally, I found Section 5.2 to be in tension with the main message prior to that section, and perhaps too preliminary to be presentable.)

--I think one other experiment that would significantly strengthen the paper is one that tests the implications mentioned for scaling limits in L107 (e.g. measure feature calibration as models are scaled from the underparametered scaling regime to overparameterized.) This would give some more diverse evidence of places where feature calibration breaks down.

Nit points:
--Typo L263: spare -> "sparse"
--Typo L265: Figure label should be "2A"
--L749 in appendix is cut off
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The paper does not address potential negative societal impact, but I believe it is not necessary given the nature of the work.

The authors do provide adequate discussion of limitations and are clear about the conjectural portions of their work.
