reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This submission proposes a new type of generalization satisfied by interpolating classifiers (the primary focus), that is different from agreement between test error and train error as in classical learning theory. This type of generalization is largely motivated by empirical evidence across different models (neural networks, kernel regression & svm, decision trees) and theoretically for 1-Nearest Neighbors classification (Theorem 1). More specifically, the submission claims that for certain types of mappings x -> L(x) (termed "distinguishable features" -- i.e. they must be learnable to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration), the joint distribution of (L(x), y) where (x,y) pairs are drawn from the true distribution will be "close" to the joint distribution of (L(x), f(x)) on the test set, where f(x) is the trained model prediction.

List of contributions:
--Proposes that interpolating classifiers satisfy "distributional generalization." This is first more specifically formulated as the "Feature Calibration Conjecture" (FCC) in Section 3. A crucial component of this is to identify the types of features L(x) for which the calibration holds (Definition 1); roughly, if the feature is learnable from inputs x to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration (fixed algorithm, data distribution, number of samples, allowable error), then under that same setting calibration will be achieved. The authors prove FCC is true for 1-Nearest neighbors. The Feature Calibration Conjecture is not fully specified, in that there is an assumption of "natural distribution" D (which is such that the conjecture holds in the empirical settings considered).

--Empirical test of the FCC. The types of distinguishable features L considered include (i) the constant partition (calibration then implies the marginal distribution p(y) of the predicted labels matches that of the true label distribution); (ii) a "coarse partition" (L(x) is an "easy" classification task) and calibration holds for fine-grained label subgroups, for instance in Imagenet; (iii) "class partition" (classes that can be learned well from standard datasets, and the true label distribution is a noisy corruption of this); (iv) multiple features. Models investigated include neural networks (WideResnets and MLPs), kernels (standard and high-performing ones), and decision trees. 

--A definition for "distributional generalization" which subsumes the definition of feature calibration & classical generalization, and very preliminary evidence that this holds for non-interpolating models (a Wide Resnet model on CIFAR-10 during training).

Main Review:
Originality: This work identifies & investigates an original empirical property that appears to be satisfied by interpolating classifiers. The proposed concepts (Feature Calibration) are general & seem distinct from any prior work to the best of my knowledge. (For example, the authors note that "standard" calibration is a particular case of Feature Calibration with specific choice of L(x).) I think this work is bold in what it seeks to accomplish (asking a fundamentally new question at the basis of learning theory, building evidence from diverse empirical examples) and makes a reasonable first step towards doing so. 

Quality: The quality of the work seems high. The authors give a number of different empirical examples with sufficient coverage across model diversity and provide a theoretically satisfied case (1-Nearest Neighbors). The authors note the limitations of the work (e.g. the FCC is specified in terms of "natural distributions" which has an unclear meaning) and are fairly thorough in addressing potential concerns & connections to other works. I was not able to identify significant shortcomings with respect to the conceptual formulation or message of the paper. I make some comments about diversity of empirical examples (with respect to datasets / partitions) below.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and was straightforward to read.

Significance: I think this paper takes a risk in its premise, provides a reasonable amount of evidence in support of its main claims, and would spur further work in this area; as I was also not able to identify any major issues with the paper, this is the reason behind my suggested score. In Section 1.3 the authors discuss a number of consequences for learning theory which seem notable. This work tells us more about the implicit bias of models (though not just neural networks); most interesting I think is that in certain limits (e.g. when models are interpolating, and model size >> dataset size), models are not approaching the Bayes optimal classifier but sample from the distribution p(y|x). 

Other comments & questions:

--I find some mentions of "Bayes optimal classifier" to be confusing at times -- for instance, its usage in L268 seems to be with respect to argmax p(y | L(x)) (rather than argmax p(y|x)), if I understand correctly. Perhaps it is worth pointing this out and the connection between the two (e.g. in the setting where p(y|x) is discussed, section starting at L107). 

--In the empirical examples with label noise, why do the authors consider only sparse confusion matrices?

--I think this work has good empirical diversity across very distinct classes of interpolating models, but one place where the paper could be noticeably strengthened is diversity of examples with respect to the datasets / partitions L(x) that are considered. Apart from the constant partition L(x) = 0, all the partitions seem to be largely based off of semantic distinctions that are already available in datasets. (Because of this, I also wondered whether the behavior is largely inherited from the dataset and from the existing semantic structure within it.) Are there more complex L(x), that are not semantically constructed, that can be considered? Separate but related to this, is it valid to consider L(x) that have some knowledge of the distribution D, or even a particular training set? For instance, L(x) that is constructed from trained intermediate layer embeddings in a neural network -- or would L(x) that has knowledge of the distribution D potentially break FCC?

(For instance, this type of L(x) / y constructed from true labels / label noise on existing label structure, e.g. CIFAR-10, is also used in the non-interpolating setting in Section 5.2, which makes the reader wonder what is actually happening in the interpolating setting. More generally, I found Section 5.2 to be in tension with the main message prior to that section, and perhaps too preliminary to be presentable.)

--I think one other experiment that would significantly strengthen the paper is one that tests the implications mentioned for scaling limits in L107 (e.g. measure feature calibration as models are scaled from the underparametered scaling regime to overparameterized.) This would give some more diverse evidence of places where feature calibration breaks down.

Nit points:
--Typo L263: spare -> "sparse"
--Typo L265: Figure label should be "2A"
--L749 in appendix is cut off

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper does not address potential negative societal impact, but I believe it is not necessary given the nature of the work.

The authors do provide adequate discussion of limitations and are clear about the conjectural portions of their work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This submission introduces and investigates a novel form of generalization, termed "distributional generalization," which is observed in interpolating classifiers. This concept stands apart from the classical understanding of generalization based on the alignment of test and training errors. The authors build their case through diverse empirical observations across various models, including neural networks, kernel methods (regression and SVM), and decision trees, complemented by a theoretical proof for 1-Nearest Neighbors classification (Theorem 1). The core assertion is that for certain mappings, denoted L(x) and referred to as "distinguishable features"—meaning they are learnable to a high test accuracy within a given context—the joint distribution of (L(x), y) (where (x,y) are drawn from the true data distribution) will closely approximate the joint distribution of (L(x), f(x)) on the test set, where f(x) represents the trained model's prediction.

**Contributions:**

*   **Introduction of "Distributional Generalization" and the "Feature Calibration Conjecture" (FCC):** The paper proposes that interpolating classifiers exhibit "distributional generalization," which is more precisely articulated as the Feature Calibration Conjecture in Section 3. A key aspect of this conjecture is the identification of "distinguishable features" L(x) (Definition 1). These are features that can be learned with good test accuracy under specific conditions (fixed algorithm, data distribution, sample size, and allowable error). The authors provide a theoretical validation of the FCC for 1-Nearest Neighbors. A noted limitation is that the FCC's full specification relies on an assumption of a "natural distribution" D, the precise meaning of which remains somewhat ambiguous.
*   **Empirical Validation of the FCC:** The study presents extensive empirical tests of the FCC using various types of distinguishable features L. These include: (i) a constant partition, L(x) = 0, where calibration implies the marginal distribution of predicted labels matches that of true labels; (ii) a "coarse partition," where L(x) represents an "easy" classification task, and calibration holds for finer-grained label subgroups (e.g., in ImageNet); (iii) a "class partition," involving classes that are well-learnable from standard datasets, with the true label distribution being a noisy corruption of this; and (iv) multiple features. The models investigated span neural networks (WideResnets and MLPs), various kernel methods, and decision trees, demonstrating broad applicability.
*   **Definition of "Distributional Generalization" and Preliminary Evidence for Non-Interpolating Models:** The paper offers a comprehensive definition for "distributional generalization" that encompasses both feature calibration and classical generalization. Furthermore, it provides initial evidence suggesting this concept might also apply to non-interpolating models, illustrated by a Wide Resnet model on CIFAR-10 during its training phase.

**Main Review:**

**Originality:** This work stands out by identifying and thoroughly investigating a novel empirical property characteristic of interpolating classifiers. The proposed concepts, particularly Feature Calibration, are broad in scope and appear distinct from existing literature, to my knowledge (the authors themselves point out that "standard" calibration can be seen as a specific instance of Feature Calibration). The paper is ambitious in its goal, posing a fundamental new question at the heart of learning theory and supporting it with diverse empirical examples, marking a commendable initial stride in this direction.

**Quality:** The overall quality of the submission is high. The authors present a wide array of empirical examples, covering a good spectrum of model architectures. The theoretical validation for 1-Nearest Neighbors further strengthens the paper. The limitations of the work, such as the vaguely defined "natural distributions" in the FCC, are openly acknowledged. The authors are diligent in addressing potential concerns and connecting their findings to related research. I found no significant conceptual flaws or weaknesses in the paper's core message. My only minor reservation concerns the breadth of empirical examples regarding datasets and partitions, which is elaborated upon below.

**Clarity:** The paper is exceptionally well-written and structured, making it straightforward and enjoyable to read.

**Significance:** The paper's premise is bold, yet it provides a compelling body of evidence to support its central claims, making it highly likely to stimulate further research in this nascent area. Given the absence of any major issues, this is a key factor in my positive assessment. Section 1.3 notably discusses several profound implications for learning theory. This research contributes significantly to our understanding of the implicit biases present in various models (not just neural networks). A particularly intriguing insight is the suggestion that in certain asymptotic regimes (e.g., when models interpolate and model size vastly exceeds dataset size), models may not converge to the Bayes optimal classifier but rather sample from the true conditional distribution p(y|x).

**Other Comments & Questions:**

*   **Clarification on Bayes Optimal Classifier:** The usage of "Bayes optimal classifier" in line L268 can be somewhat confusing. It appears to refer to `argmax p(y | L(x))` rather than the more conventional `argmax p(y|x)`. It would be beneficial to explicitly clarify this distinction and elaborate on the relationship between these two interpretations, perhaps within the section discussing `p(y|x)` (starting at L107).
*   **Label Noise and Sparse Confusion Matrices:** Could the authors elaborate on the rationale behind exclusively considering sparse confusion matrices in the empirical examples involving label noise?
*   **Diversity of Datasets and Partitions:** While the paper demonstrates commendable empirical diversity across various interpolating model classes, it could be substantially strengthened by broadening the range of datasets and partitions L(x) considered. Beyond the constant partition L(x) = 0, most partitions appear to be derived from pre-existing semantic distinctions within datasets. This raises a question as to whether the observed behavior might largely stem from the inherent semantic structure of the datasets themselves. Exploring more complex L(x) that are not semantically pre-constructed would be valuable. Furthermore, it would be insightful to discuss the validity of using L(x) that incorporates some knowledge of the true distribution D or even the specific training set (e.g., L(x) derived from intermediate layer embeddings of a trained neural network)—and whether such an approach might potentially invalidate the Feature Calibration Conjecture.
    *   Related to this, the construction of L(x)/y from true labels/label noise on existing dataset structures (e.g., CIFAR-10) is also employed in the non-interpolating setting in Section 5.2. This overlap can lead readers to question the specific insights gained from the interpolating setting. More generally, Section 5.2 felt somewhat preliminary and potentially in tension with the primary message of the preceding sections.
*   **Scaling Limits Experiment:** A significant addition to the paper would be an experiment testing the implications discussed in L107 regarding scaling limits. For instance, measuring feature calibration as models transition from underparameterized to overparameterized regimes would provide valuable and diverse evidence, particularly illuminating scenarios where feature calibration might cease to hold.

**Nit Points:**

*   Typo at L263: "spare" should be "sparse."
*   Typo at L265: The figure label should correctly read "2A."
*   Content at L749 in the appendix appears to be truncated.

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**

The paper does not delve into potential negative societal impacts, which is appropriate given the foundational nature of the research. The authors provide an adequate discussion of the work's limitations and are commendably transparent about the conjectural aspects of their findings.

**Rating:** 7: Good paper, accept.

reviews.summary: The authors consider interpolating classifiers, i.e. classifiers that interpolate the training data by selecting the correct class for training samples, often performing poorly in terms of generalization error. The authors propose a new notion of generalization, i.e. the Distributional Generalization. Such a notion does not focus on the average error over the inputs, and subsumes both classical generalization and the conjecture that the outputs of classifiers match the statistics of their training distribution when conditioned on certain subgroups. The authors formally introduce and experimentally validate a conjecture to specify such subgroups. While the conjecture is proven for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, empirical evidence is provided for other classifiers; such as kernel machines and neural networks.
reviews.main_review:
== Main comments ==

- The paper is overall well written and the topics are clearly exposed. Illustrative examples are effectively presented to further clarify the problem at hand.
- The presented work tackles a timely and relevant problem in machine learning, drawing ideas from many related areas of research, such as output distribution calibration, overparametrization, and benign overfitting. The presented results are worthy but not completely surprising. 
- The paper addresses a very much similar problem than : 
Vapnik, V. & Izmailov, R.. (2020). Complete statistical theory of learning: learning using statistical invariants. Proceedings of the Ninth Symposium on Conformal and Probabilistic Prediction and Applications, in PMLR 128:4-40
However, this problem is known to below to the class of  ill-posed problems. How does the current work related to the above mentioned reference? How does it overcome the ill-posed problem. 
- In eq. (1) the notation is a bit confusing. Are TrainSet and TestSet the distributions from which the samples of these sets are drawn (but then D_tr, D_te are used to mean the same), or rather the sets themselves (and then ~ should be replaced with ∈)?
- One of the two main contributions is thoroughly expressed in section 3.2. This is indeed a very interesting observation. However it looks closely related to the problem defined in section 2 of [26].
Could the authors further underline the value and originality of their contribution by discussing the connection between the two concepts?
- Although the feature calibration conjecture is fully proved to 1-nearest-neighbors classifiers, it is also true that it represents a very limited model. It seems that for more complex (and more commonly used) models, only empirical evidence can be provided so far, with no much theoretical formalization. Although this is not necessarily a negative aspect for works in progress and contributions which bring new ideas, the expectation set up by the writers in the introductory section are diminished by lack of formalization.
- The training procedure is sometimes indicated as Train_A, and sometimes as A. We suggest to choose one symbol and consistently use it throughout the whole paper.
- Have the authors thought of any potential issue related to identifying potentially sensitive subgroups L such as, ethnicity, high/low income, etc. instead of dogs, cats, etc. when using different
datasets?
- We would like to draw attention on the fact that, in line 101, the reference to the work in [7] does not seem to properly fit it. Indeed, in [7] the authors already explain that ‘benign overfitting’ is only characterized in specific cases and does not generally apply, making the claim in lines 103-104 appear a bit excessive. Reference [26] should be reported as the version officially published at ICML 2017.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors take into the scientific limitations of their work in section 3.4. Further improvements can be implemented by answering the questions and doubts raised in the Main Review.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**

This paper investigates interpolating classifiers, which perfectly fit training data but frequently exhibit suboptimal generalization performance. The authors introduce a novel concept termed "Distributional Generalization," which departs from the traditional focus on average error across inputs. This notion is presented as encompassing both classical generalization and the observation that classifier outputs align with the statistical characteristics of their training distribution when conditioned on specific subgroups. The study formally proposes and empirically validates a conjecture for identifying these relevant subgroups. While the conjecture is theoretically proven for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, empirical evidence is provided to suggest its applicability to more complex models, such as kernel machines and neural networks.

**Main Assessment:**

The manuscript is generally well-structured and lucidly presented, making its complex subject matter accessible. The inclusion of illustrative examples significantly aids in clarifying the core problem addressed. The research addresses a pertinent and timely challenge within machine learning, integrating insights from diverse fields such as output distribution calibration, overparameterization, and benign overfitting. While the findings contribute valuable perspectives to the field, their overall impact is somewhat tempered by a sense of being an expected, rather than groundbreaking, development.

A primary concern arises from the apparent overlap with existing literature. The problem explored here bears a strong resemblance to the "complete statistical theory of learning" framework detailed in Vapnik & Izmailov (2020), which specifically addresses learning using statistical invariants. Given that this class of problems is frequently characterized as ill-posed, it is crucial for the authors to elaborate on how their current methodology relates to this prior work and, more importantly, how it effectively navigates or resolves the inherent ill-posedness.

Furthermore, the exposition in equation (1) could benefit from greater clarity. The use of "TrainSet" and "TestSet" in conjunction with `D_tr` and `D_te` for distributions is potentially confusing. It is unclear whether "TrainSet" and "TestSet" denote the underlying distributions (in which case `D_tr` and `D_te` would be redundant or used inconsistently) or the actual sample sets themselves (in which case the `~` symbol should likely be replaced with `∈` to denote membership).

One of the paper's key contributions, elaborated in Section 3.2, presents an intriguing observation. However, its conceptual proximity to the problem formulation in Section 2 of reference [26] necessitates further clarification. To fully establish the distinct value and originality of their contribution, the authors should provide a more thorough discussion delineating the precise connections and divergences between their concept and that presented in [26].

A significant limitation lies in the theoretical underpinning of the feature calibration conjecture. While a complete proof is offered for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, this represents a relatively simplistic model. For more advanced and widely utilized models, the paper primarily offers empirical evidence without substantial theoretical formalization. This disparity between the theoretical depth for a rudimentary model and the empirical-only support for complex ones somewhat diminishes the ambitious expectations set forth in the introductory sections regarding the scope of the work.

Minor inconsistencies in notation were also noted; for instance, the training procedure is sometimes denoted as `Train_A` and at other times simply as `A`. Adopting a single, consistent symbol throughout the manuscript would enhance readability.

The authors might also consider the broader implications of identifying sensitive subgroups. While the examples provided (e.g., dogs, cats) are innocuous, the framework could potentially be applied to categorize individuals based on sensitive attributes such as ethnicity or socioeconomic status. A brief discussion on the ethical considerations or potential pitfalls of such applications, especially when using different datasets, would be valuable.

Finally, specific references require attention. The citation of [7] in line 101 appears to be misplaced, as [7] explicitly states that 'benign overfitting' is context-specific and not a general phenomenon, which contradicts the broader assertion made in lines 103-104. Additionally, reference [26] should be updated to cite its officially published version from ICML 2017.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**

The authors appropriately acknowledge some scientific limitations in Section 3.4. Addressing the various questions and concerns raised in this review, particularly regarding theoretical depth, related work, and clarity, would significantly enhance the paper's overall robustness and impact.

**Rating:**

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors consider interpolating classifiers, i.e. classifiers that interpolate the training data by selecting the correct class for training samples, often performing poorly in terms of generalization error. The authors propose a new notion of generalization, i.e. the Distributional Generalization. Such a notion does not focus on the average error over the inputs, and subsumes both classical generalization and the conjecture that the outputs of classifiers match the statistics of their training distribution when conditioned on certain subgroups. The authors formally introduce and experimentally validate a conjecture to specify such subgroups. While the conjecture is proven for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, empirical evidence is provided for other classifiers; such as kernel machines and neural networks.

Main Review:
== Main comments ==

- The paper is overall well written and the topics are clearly exposed. Illustrative examples are effectively presented to further clarify the problem at hand.
- The presented work tackles a timely and relevant problem in machine learning, drawing ideas from many related areas of research, such as output distribution calibration, overparametrization, and benign overfitting. The presented results are worthy but not completely surprising. 
- The paper addresses a very much similar problem than : 
Vapnik, V. & Izmailov, R.. (2020). Complete statistical theory of learning: learning using statistical invariants. Proceedings of the Ninth Symposium on Conformal and Probabilistic Prediction and Applications, in PMLR 128:4-40
However, this problem is known to below to the class of  ill-posed problems. How does the current work related to the above mentioned reference? How does it overcome the ill-posed problem. 
- In eq. (1) the notation is a bit confusing. Are TrainSet and TestSet the distributions from which the samples of these sets are drawn (but then D_tr, D_te are used to mean the same), or rather the sets themselves (and then ~ should be replaced with ∈)?
- One of the two main contributions is thoroughly expressed in section 3.2. This is indeed a very interesting observation. However it looks closely related to the problem defined in section 2 of [26].
Could the authors further underline the value and originality of their contribution by discussing the connection between the two concepts?
- Although the feature calibration conjecture is fully proved to 1-nearest-neighbors classifiers, it is also true that it represents a very limited model. It seems that for more complex (and more commonly used) models, only empirical evidence can be provided so far, with no much theoretical formalization. Although this is not necessarily a negative aspect for works in progress and contributions which bring new ideas, the expectation set up by the writers in the introductory section are diminished by lack of formalization.
- The training procedure is sometimes indicated as Train_A, and sometimes as A. We suggest to choose one symbol and consistently use it throughout the whole paper.
- Have the authors thought of any potential issue related to identifying potentially sensitive subgroups L such as, ethnicity, high/low income, etc. instead of dogs, cats, etc. when using different
datasets?
- We would like to draw attention on the fact that, in line 101, the reference to the work in [7] does not seem to properly fit it. Indeed, in [7] the authors already explain that ‘benign overfitting’ is only characterized in specific cases and does not generally apply, making the claim in lines 103-104 appear a bit excessive. Reference [26] should be reported as the version officially published at ICML 2017.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors take into the scientific limitations of their work in section 3.4. Further improvements can be implemented by answering the questions and doubts raised in the Main Review.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Distributed Learning with Strategic Users: A Repeated Game Approach

reviews.summary: The paper studies a federated learning scheme for training machine learning models using gradients provided by a set of strategic users. At each iteration, each user gets a mini-batch of samples, computes her gradient, and decides whether to report it to the fusion center (FC) or to report a random signal.  The authors propose a strategy for the FC which incentivizes users to truthfully report their gradients and ensure convergence guarantees. Results are validated experimentally on publicly available datasets.
reviews.main_review:
Federated learning is a relevant problem in machine learning and I believe this work positively contributes to the existing literature. 
The work adds both novel theoretical contributions and experimental results. Moreover, it is clearly written. Hence, my acceptance score.  However, I have a few comments for the authors: 

- It would be nice to have related works discussed in the main paper, clearly highlighting the differences in terms of set-ups and assumptions considered. I believe the problem of federated learning can be tackled from a lot of different perspectives and it would be nice to have this clear from the first sections. A lot of technical definitions and details could be moved to the Appendix instead (e.g., Equation 20).

- In the experiments, only a static strategy vector is considered for the users. However, it would be natural to consider users who adapt their strategy online. Is there a particular reason why this was not considered? This would test whether the FC algorithm actually steers the agents to collaborate in the long run.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I don't see potential negative societal impact for this work.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a federated learning framework designed to train machine learning models by integrating gradients contributed by a collective of strategic users. In each iterative step, individual users process a mini-batch of data, compute their respective gradients, and then decide whether to transmit this genuine gradient to the fusion center (FC) or to submit a randomized signal instead. The authors put forth a novel strategy for the FC that is engineered to motivate users to report their true gradients, while simultaneously guaranteeing the convergence of the learning process. The proposed methodology is empirically validated using publicly available datasets.

**Main Review:**
Federated learning represents a highly pertinent research area within machine learning, and I am convinced that this submission offers a valuable contribution to the existing body of literature. The work is commendable for its inclusion of both original theoretical advancements and supportive experimental findings. Furthermore, the manuscript is presented with notable clarity. These strengths collectively lead to my recommendation for acceptance. However, I do have a couple of points that the authors might consider for improvement:

*   It would significantly enhance the paper if a dedicated section discussing related works were integrated into the main body of the text. This discussion should clearly delineate the distinctions in the experimental setups and underlying assumptions compared to existing approaches. Given the multifaceted nature of federated learning problems, providing this contextual clarity early on would be highly beneficial for the reader. Conversely, certain highly technical definitions and detailed derivations (such as Equation 20) could be more appropriately placed in an Appendix to improve the flow of the main narrative.

*   Regarding the experimental evaluation, I noted that only a static strategy vector was employed for the participating users. It seems more realistic and informative to explore scenarios where users dynamically adapt their strategies over time. Could the authors elaborate on the rationale behind opting for a static model in this context? Investigating adaptive user behaviors would provide a more rigorous test of whether the FC algorithm can indeed guide agents towards sustained collaborative behavior in the long run.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
I do not perceive any immediate negative societal implications arising from the research presented in this work.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper formulates the interaction between the fusion center (FC) and users in distributed learning (or federated learning) from the perspective of game theory. In order to encourage the users to provide effective information to the FC for model update, the FC adopt zero-determinant (ZD) strategy, associated with which there are some practical issues addressed by the authors. Theoretical analysis on the convergence of the proposed algorithm is presented.
reviews.main_review:
The paper formulates the interaction between the fusion center (FC) and users in distributed learning (or federated learning) from the perspective of game theory. In order to encourage the users to provide effective information to the FC for model update, the FC adopt zero-determinant (ZD) strategy, associated with which there are some practical issues addressed by the authors. Theoretical analysis on the convergence of the proposed algorithm is presented. The formulation and the results are interesting; however, there are some concerns from the reviewer:

1.	What’s the motivation of the users submitting random noninformative signal to the FC instead of just submitting nothing when they decide not to cooperate. Submitting random noninformative signal does not benefit the users but harms the FC. 
2.	Please explain the state transition matrix $\Omega$ in eq. (3), why it is constructed as it is and why the states are supposed to evolve according to it. Just below eq. (3), the authors use \bar{\Omega} to denote the transition matrix, what is the difference between $\Omega$ and $\bar{\Omega}$, or it is just a typo?
3.	The authors are expected to clearly state the goal of users in playing the game, namely, in each iteration, the users maximize what, the expected long-term reward, or the reward of this iteration. Based on the objective of the users, the author shall show that the users’ best response is q* = [1, 1, 1, 1]^T given that the FC adopts ZD strategy. 
4.	In the Experiments section, the figure referred is Fig 2 rather than Fig 4. SGSU is SGD-SU, typo? Does Pavlov mean the win-stay-lose-switch? If it does, please just use one of these two phrases throughout the paper. 
5.	From Figure 2(a) and (b), it is shown that the optimality gap is the smallest when the users take tit-for-tat strategy. Does this mean the FC prefers tit-for-tat instead of full-cooperation in order to obtain a favorable solution to optimization problem (1)? This is not consistent with the game, where the FC prefers full-cooperation? 
6.	Since the problem is formulated as a repeated game, the experiments shall show whether the strategy of the users converges exactly to q* starting from any initial state and action. 
7.	The reviewer believes (24) admits a closed-form solution, and the computational complexity of (24) is the same as that of (6). Please check this.

reviews.text:
**Summary:**
This paper explores the strategic interaction between a fusion center (FC) and individual users within a distributed (or federated) learning framework, utilizing a game-theoretic approach. To incentivize users to contribute valuable data for model updates, the FC employs a zero-determinant (ZD) strategy. The authors also tackle several practical challenges associated with this strategy and provide a theoretical analysis of the proposed algorithm's convergence properties.

**Main Review:**
The manuscript presents an intriguing application of game theory to model the dynamics between a fusion center and its participating users in a distributed learning environment. The core idea involves the FC leveraging a zero-determinant (ZD) strategy to encourage users to provide high-quality information for model improvement. The authors also delve into practical considerations and offer a theoretical examination of the algorithm's convergence. While the overall formulation and the presented findings are noteworthy, I have several points that require clarification and revision:

1.  **User Motivation for Non-Cooperation:** It is unclear why users, when opting not to cooperate, would submit random, non-informative signals instead of simply refraining from submission. Submitting arbitrary data appears to offer no benefit to the user while actively hindering the FC's process. The rationale behind this specific form of non-cooperation needs further elaboration.
2.  **State Transition Matrix Elaboration:** A more comprehensive explanation is needed for the construction and theoretical justification of the state transition matrix $\Omega$ in Equation (3). Specifically, why are the states expected to evolve according to this particular matrix? Furthermore, a distinction or clarification is required regarding the notation $\bar{\Omega}$ used immediately after Equation (3); please confirm if this is a distinct matrix or a typographical error.
3.  **User Objective and Best Response:** The paper should explicitly define the users' objective function in the game. Do users aim to maximize their expected long-term reward, or their reward for the current iteration? Based on this clarified objective, the authors must rigorously demonstrate that q* = [1, 1, 1, 1]^T indeed represents the users' optimal response when the FC employs the ZD strategy.
4.  **Experimental Section Details:** Several points in the experimental section require attention. It appears that Figure 2 is referenced as Figure 4 in the text, which should be corrected. The acronym "SGSU" needs to be fully defined or confirmed as "SGD-SU." Additionally, if "Pavlov" refers to the "win-stay-lose-switch" strategy, it is advisable to use a single, consistent term throughout the paper for clarity.
5.  **FC's Preferred Strategy in Experiments:** Figures 2(a) and (b) indicate that the optimality gap is minimized when users adopt a tit-for-tat strategy. This observation raises a question: Does this imply that the FC benefits more from users employing tit-for-tat rather than full cooperation to achieve a favorable solution to optimization problem (1)? This finding seems to contradict the game's underlying premise, where the FC would ideally prefer full cooperation.
6.  **Convergence of User Strategy in Experiments:** Given the formulation as a repeated game, the experimental results should ideally illustrate whether the users' strategy consistently converges to q* irrespective of their initial state and actions. This demonstration would strengthen the empirical validation of the proposed model.
7.  **Computational Aspects of Equation (24):** I suspect that Equation (24) might admit a closed-form solution. The authors should verify this and also provide a comparison of its computational complexity relative to that of Equation (6).

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The paper does not identify any negative societal impacts, which seems appropriate given the technical nature of the work.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposed an incentive mechanism for a distributed learning setting. The users are encouraged but not obliged to provide the gradient. The results on convergence and an upper bound on the estimation bias of the gradient were shown.
reviews.main_review:
I am mostly concerned about the setup (line 64-line 74). 

“In iteration t, when a user cooperates, he provides an information gain R to the FC at his privacy cost VUR with 0”. It is unclear to me how the authors quantifies the privacy cost and why it is related to the information gain R via a factor V_U between 0 and 1. It seems to me that the information gain R was not used in Algorithm 1. Would the authors explain how R is used? Moreover, the information gain R should vary every time. Is it the case in the paper’s setup?

“The FC is cooperative (Ak,t=C) if she makes a payment r to the user at her cost rVFC with 0”. Why is the cost of the FC rV_{FC} related to V_{FC} via a factor V_FC between 0 and 1? Would the authors explain the intuition behind this?

line 72-73: the authors did not specify how each entry in a payoff vector is ordered.

It would be beneficial to discuss when Phi_t/Psi_t>=p1/p2, Phi_t/Psi_t>=p3/p4 are satisfied and if they are always satisfied (line 151). 

I like it that Theorem 2 gives a bound on \zeta_t. But there are still some unclear things. 

how large is $\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \alpha_i^2$ compared to $\eta^2$? The authors should establish that the bound (22a) would not be vacuous. Recall in line 61 that the norm of the gradient is <= \ell. This bound is vacuous if the upper bound is just close to \ell. The authors may want to compute the expected value of \zeta_t and then check if the bound is vacuous. 

What is $\Delta_{i,t}$?

The current form looks pretty complicated. The authors may want to do some asymptotic analysis and present the asymptotic order of each term so that the presentation of the results can also be simplified.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This manuscript introduces an incentive mechanism tailored for a distributed learning environment. In this proposed setup, users are encouraged, though not strictly required, to contribute their gradients. The authors present theoretical results concerning convergence properties and establish an upper bound for the estimation bias of the gradient.

**Main Review:**
My primary concerns largely center on the clarity and justification of the system's foundational setup, particularly as described in lines 64-74.

The statement, "In iteration t, when a user cooperates, he provides an information gain R to the FC at his privacy cost VUR with 0," raises several questions. It is not immediately clear how this "privacy cost" (VUR) is quantitatively determined. Specifically, the rationale behind establishing a direct relationship between this cost and the "information gain R" via a scaling factor V_U (constrained between 0 and 1) requires further explanation. Moreover, the "information gain R" itself does not appear to be explicitly integrated or utilized within Algorithm 1. The authors should clarify its role and usage within the proposed mechanism. Additionally, it would be beneficial to understand if this information gain R is considered a dynamic variable, changing across iterations, and how such variability is handled within the paper's framework.

A similar point of clarification is needed for the Federated Coordinator's (FC's) side: "The FC is cooperative (Ak,t=C) if she makes a payment r to the user at her cost rVFC with 0". The formulation of the FC's cost as rV_FC, where V_FC is another scaling factor between 0 and 1, lacks an intuitive explanation. Providing the underlying reasoning or intuition for this cost structure would significantly enhance understanding.

Furthermore, in lines 72-73, the authors have omitted a crucial detail regarding the ordering of entries within the payoff vector, which is essential for a complete and unambiguous understanding of the game-theoretic interactions.

Beyond the setup, it would be highly advantageous to include a discussion on the conditions under which the inequalities Phi_t/Psi_t >= p1/p2 and Phi_t/Psi_t >= p3/p4 are satisfied (line 151). Clarifying whether these conditions are universally met or apply only under specific circumstances would strengthen the theoretical contribution.

While Theorem 2 offers a valuable bound on \zeta_t, certain aspects of its presentation and implications could be further clarified. A key point is the relative magnitude of $\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \alpha_i^2$ compared to $\eta^2$. The authors must provide assurances that the derived bound (22a) is not trivial or "vacuous." Given the previous statement in line 61 that the norm of the gradient is bounded by \ell, the bound would offer limited insight if its upper limit merely approaches \ell. I recommend performing a calculation of the expected value of \zeta_t to rigorously confirm the non-vacuous nature of the bound.

Additionally, the term $\Delta_{i,t}$ is introduced without a clear and explicit definition, which impedes a full comprehension of the bound's constituent elements.

Finally, the current mathematical presentation of some results appears quite complex. To enhance clarity and highlight the core insights, I suggest performing an asymptotic analysis. Presenting the asymptotic order of each term would considerably simplify the overall presentation of the results, making them more accessible and impactful.

**Rating:**
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary:
This paper proposed an incentive mechanism for a distributed learning setting. The users are encouraged but not obliged to provide the gradient. The results on convergence and an upper bound on the estimation bias of the gradient were shown.

Main Review:
I am mostly concerned about the setup (line 64-line 74). 

“In iteration t, when a user cooperates, he provides an information gain R to the FC at his privacy cost VUR with 0”. It is unclear to me how the authors quantifies the privacy cost and why it is related to the information gain R via a factor V_U between 0 and 1. It seems to me that the information gain R was not used in Algorithm 1. Would the authors explain how R is used? Moreover, the information gain R should vary every time. Is it the case in the paper’s setup?

“The FC is cooperative (Ak,t=C) if she makes a payment r to the user at her cost rVFC with 0”. Why is the cost of the FC rV_{FC} related to V_{FC} via a factor V_FC between 0 and 1? Would the authors explain the intuition behind this?

line 72-73: the authors did not specify how each entry in a payoff vector is ordered.

It would be beneficial to discuss when Phi_t/Psi_t>=p1/p2, Phi_t/Psi_t>=p3/p4 are satisfied and if they are always satisfied (line 151). 

I like it that Theorem 2 gives a bound on \zeta_t. But there are still some unclear things. 

how large is $\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \alpha_i^2$ compared to $\eta^2$? The authors should establish that the bound (22a) would not be vacuous. Recall in line 61 that the norm of the gradient is <= \ell. This bound is vacuous if the upper bound is just close to \ell. The authors may want to compute the expected value of \zeta_t and then check if the bound is vacuous. 

What is $\Delta_{i,t}$?

The current form looks pretty complicated. The authors may want to do some asymptotic analysis and present the asymptotic order of each term so that the presentation of the results can also be simplified.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the incentives in distributed learning, where the users can choose to join the cooperation or not. The paper formalizes this problem as a two-player game between the fusion center and the user, designs a strategy to learn and provides a convergence guarantee. The contributions include: 1. formalizing of a reward mechanism between FC and users; 2. proposing a strategy for the FC to make users join the cooperation, where FC cannot observe users' actions; 3. providing theoretical guarantee on the convergence rate.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: The task is new. The problem formalization and the mechanism proposed are novel.

Quality: The submission is technically sound. But the assumptions are somewhat unreasonable to me and I would like to know if these assumptions are necessary and non-vacuous. 

Specifically, for Assumption 1, it might be uncommon to make assumptions over the gradient estimate, which is a r.v. dependent on the objective function and the noise distributions. I am curious if the assumption could be made directly over the objective function and the distributions instead of the gradient estimate. For Assumption 2, $\tilde{\Lambda}_t$ depends on $\Lambda_t$, the objective function and the noise distributions. Similar to Assumption 1, it looks quite strange to make direct assumptions over $\tilde{\Lambda}_t$.  Also, before Assumption 2, the authors state that ``" $\Lambda_t q^\top$ and $\tilde{\Lambda}_t q^\top$ must meet the following criteria ...". Is Assumption 2 a necessary condition? 

Besides, the gradient estimator $\hat m_t$ depends on $q$. It is unclear to me what $q$ is.  Is it the user's initial strategy? Is it deterministic?

Clarity: Some notations are unclear. It would be better if the paper has more explanation on the assumptions.

Significance: The problem studied by this paper is interesting and important.

Other comments:
1. What is $Q$ in Eq. (8)?
2. Is $\hat {m}_t^\top (m_t - \hat m_t)$ in Assumption 1 always positive? If not, $\sqrt{\hat m_t^\top (m_t - \hat m_t)}$  is not well-defined.
3. Typo: $Ray(\hat m_t, \Sigma_t)$ should be $Ray(\Sigma_t,\hat m_t)$.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No. The authors are recommended to add a discussion on the societal impact.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This manuscript delves into the study of incentive mechanisms within distributed learning environments, particularly focusing on scenarios where individual users have the autonomy to decide whether to engage in cooperative tasks. The authors frame this challenge as a two-player game, involving a central fusion entity and a participating user, and subsequently propose a learning strategy accompanied by a theoretical convergence guarantee. The principal contributions of the paper include: (1) the formalization of a reward structure between the fusion center and its users; (2) the development of a strategy for the fusion center to encourage user cooperation, even in situations where user actions are unobservable; and (3) the provision of a rigorous theoretical analysis concerning the convergence rate of the proposed method.

**Main Review:**

**Originality:** The core problem addressed by this paper is novel. Both the formalization of this problem and the proposed mechanism to tackle it demonstrate a high degree of originality.

**Quality:** The technical execution presented in the submission is generally sound. However, I find several underlying assumptions somewhat unconvincing and would appreciate further justification regarding their necessity and whether they represent non-vacuous conditions.

Specifically, for Assumption 1, it appears unconventional to directly assume properties about the gradient estimate, given its nature as a random variable influenced by the objective function and noise distributions. I am curious whether it would be more appropriate to formulate this assumption directly on the objective function and the underlying distributions instead. Furthermore, it is critical to clarify whether the term $\hat {m}_t^\top (m_t - \hat m_t)$ in Assumption 1 is consistently positive, as its non-positivity would lead to the square root term being ill-defined. Similarly, Assumption 2, which involves $\tilde{\Lambda}_t$ (a variable dependent on $\Lambda_t$, the objective function, and noise distributions), also seems to make a direct assumption on a composite variable, mirroring the concerns raised for Assumption 1. Additionally, the statement preceding Assumption 2, which states that "$\Lambda_t q^\top$ and $\tilde{\Lambda}_t q^\top$ must meet the following criteria...", prompts a question about whether Assumption 2 is presented as a necessary consequence of these criteria.

Moreover, the gradient estimator $\hat m_t$ is noted to depend on $q$, but the identity and role of $q$ remain ambiguous. It would be helpful to clarify if $q$ represents the user's initial strategy and whether it is a deterministic or stochastic quantity.

**Clarity:** The paper suffers from some notational ambiguities, which impede clear understanding. For instance, the definition of $Q$ in Equation (8) is missing. A more thorough explanation and motivation for the various assumptions would significantly enhance the clarity of the presentation.

**Significance:** The problem investigated in this manuscript is both intrinsically interesting and holds considerable practical importance.

**Other Comments:**
*   A minor typographical error was observed: $Ray(\hat m_t, \Sigma_t)$ should be corrected to $Ray(\Sigma_t,\hat m_t)$.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The current submission does not include a discussion on the potential societal implications or limitations of the proposed work. It is strongly recommended that the authors incorporate a dedicated section addressing these important aspects.

**Rating:**
**5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold**

title: Reinforcement learning for instance segmentation with high-level priors

reviews.summary: This paper presents a strategy for object instance segmentation from images using training data that has no manual annotations. Instead of manual annotations, as is required for many state-of-the-art methods, this paper proposes to use high-level priors represented as rules that can be evaluated in the produced segmentation. These rules define what an acceptable segmentation is, and are used to create a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework formulates an environment, an agent, an action space and a reward system in a stateless system to guide segmentation. All the components of the system are well designed and well thought, and this approach may have wide applications in bioimage segmentation problems.
reviews.main_review:
* The stateless actor-critic model has connections with the dynamics of Generative Adversarial Networks, as the authors note. The fact that there is no state also begs the question of what are the connections with the REINFORCE rule for training models when the loss function is non-differentiable, and how that approach could simplify or enhance the proposed formulation.
* The experimental design is interesting and overall complete. However, the performance metrics used for evaluation are not in line with research in the instance segmentation field, where precision and recall are used in several different ways. For example, computing precision or F1 score at specific intersection over union thresholds would be more informative to understand how many objects / regions are correctly segmented and to what level of overlap with ground truth.
* There are a few datasets studied in the instance segmentation literature that could have been used as benchmarks in this evaluation. At least one dataset used for nucleus segmentation of cells, for instance, would add a lot of value to the paper for two reasons: first, it connects with previous literature in the topic, second, it allows to assess how far a model trained without ground truth data would go for segmenting these objects. It is not expected that the proposed model should be better than fully supervised approaches, but being the first in the unsupervised regime would help understand how far we are from a competitive model while not requiring ground truth data.
* The paper is overall well written and clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No comments about limitations. Positive societal impacts expected, as less annotations would be required from experts to obtain accurate models.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Main Review:

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces an innovative strategy for object instance segmentation that uniquely operates without the need for manual annotations in its training data. Unlike many state-of-the-art techniques that depend on extensive labeled datasets, this work proposes the use of high-level domain priors, formalized as rules, which can be directly evaluated against the generated segmentations. These rules serve to define what constitutes an "acceptable" segmentation and are subsequently translated into a reward signal for a reinforcement learning agent. The framework is meticulously designed, encompassing an environment, an agent, an action space, and a reward system within a stateless architecture to guide the segmentation process. The individual components of this system are thoughtfully conceived and robustly implemented, suggesting significant potential for broad application, especially within the challenging field of bioimage segmentation.

Main Review:
*   The authors appropriately note the conceptual links between their stateless actor-critic model and the operational dynamics observed in Generative Adversarial Networks. However, the inherent stateless nature of their formulation naturally prompts a deeper exploration into its connections with the REINFORCE algorithm, particularly in the context of training models where the loss function is non-differentiable. Investigating how REINFORCE might either simplify the current approach or enhance its overall effectiveness would be a valuable addition to the discussion.
*   The experimental design is commendable for its interesting approach and overall completeness. Nevertheless, a minor point of critique concerns the chosen performance metrics, which do not entirely align with the common evaluation standards within the instance segmentation research community. This field typically relies on various interpretations of precision and recall. For instance, reporting precision or F1 score at specific Intersection over Union (IoU) thresholds would offer a more comprehensive and informative understanding of both the quantity of correctly identified objects/regions and the accuracy of their spatial overlap with ground truth.
*   To further strengthen the evaluation, the paper would greatly benefit from incorporating at least one well-established benchmark dataset from the existing instance segmentation literature. Specifically, the inclusion of a dataset commonly used for cell nucleus segmentation, for example, would significantly enhance the paper's relevance. This would serve a dual purpose: first, it would foster clearer connections with prior work in the domain, and second, it would provide a crucial baseline for assessing the capabilities of a model trained entirely without ground truth data. While it is not expected for the proposed model to outperform fully supervised methods, being a pioneering effort in the unsupervised paradigm, such a comparison would be instrumental in gauging the current performance gap and understanding the potential for competitive results without relying on manual labeling.
*   The manuscript is generally well-composed and articulated with commendable clarity.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations, and none are immediately apparent that would significantly diminish the work's value. Regarding societal impact, the proposed methodology holds considerable promise for positive implications, primarily by substantially reducing the need for costly and time-consuming expert annotations to develop effective segmentation models.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper presents a RL approach for instance segmentation which formulates the instance segmentation problem as graph partitioning and the actor critic predicts the edge weights driven by the rewards, which are based on the conformity of segmented instances to high-level priors on object shape, position and size.
reviews.main_review:
This paper presents a novel idea of applying RL methodology to instance segmentation. The stateless actor-critic reinforcement learning theme enables non-differential rewards which could incorporate prior knowledge to guide the segmentation, and relaxes the heavy requirement of pixel-wise labeling. The idea is unique and quite interesting.
The related work looks sufficient to me. The experiments are well designed with convincing results.
The writing is overall clear, but there are a few parts which could be improved:
1. For Section3, it's not clear to me the essential difference between Method 1 & 2. It'd be nice to include a graph as Figure 1 to demonstrate Method 2, instead of only including it in Appendix.
2. There is a lot of valuable information in Appendix. It'd be nice to figure out a way to shorten some parts of Method section (such as Section 3.2 and experiments) and include some content from Appendix here.
3. For the experiments, some failure cases and analysis are necessary. It'd be good to add a section of ablation studies.

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel reinforcement learning (RL) framework for instance segmentation. The core idea involves formulating the instance segmentation problem as a graph partitioning task, where an actor-critic model is employed to predict edge weights. The reward signals guiding this learning process are derived from how well the segmented instances conform to high-level prior knowledge regarding object attributes such as shape, position, and size.

**Main Review:**
The authors present a truly innovative approach by applying reinforcement learning methodologies to the challenge of instance segmentation. A key strength of this work lies in the use of a stateless actor-critic architecture, which uniquely enables the incorporation of non-differentiable rewards. This flexibility is particularly advantageous as it allows the integration of valuable prior knowledge to guide the segmentation process, thereby alleviating the demanding requirement for precise pixel-wise labeling typically seen in this field. The fundamental concept underpinning this research is both original and highly engaging.

I find the coverage of related work to be comprehensive and adequate. Furthermore, the experimental design is robust, and the results presented are convincing and supportive of the proposed method.

While the overall clarity of the writing is good, there are a few specific sections that could be improved for enhanced understanding and presentation:

1.  Regarding Section 3, the essential differences between "Method 1" and "Method 2" are not immediately apparent. I suggest including a graphical representation, perhaps as an updated Figure 1, to visually demonstrate "Method 2," rather than confining this important visualization to the Appendix.
2.  A significant amount of valuable detail is currently housed within the Appendix. It would greatly benefit the reader if some of this content could be integrated into the main body of the paper. This might be achieved by judiciously condensing certain parts of the Methodology section (e.g., Section 3.2) and aspects of the experimental descriptions.
3.  To further strengthen the experimental evaluation, the paper would benefit from the inclusion of an analysis of failure cases, providing insights into the model's limitations. Additionally, a dedicated section presenting ablation studies would be highly beneficial for understanding the contribution of individual components of the proposed system.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors proposed a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for instance segmentation with superpixels. The proposed framework is based on a stateless actor-critic setup and is demonstrated to be effective on the instance segmentation tasks for one toy dataset and one microscopy dataset.
reviews.main_review:
+: strength
-: weakness 

1)Originality: 

+: This paper proposes to solve the instance segmentation tasks with weak supervision (superpixels) using the RL based methods. Different from the typical weakly supervised image instance segmentation methods, the RL-based one is a new and interesting application.

-: The authors only talked about the related work on RL however ignore the related work on instance segmentation (fully supervised and weakly supervised). 

2) Quality:

+: The proposed modules are proved to be effective via the ablation studies on the microscopy dataset. In addition, the authors also provide sufficient analysis on the selection of the hyper-parameters, such as the number of feature channels, random seed, etc.

-: There lack extensive experiments on public instance segmentation benchmark datasets, such as MS COCO [a]. In addition, there are also many instance segmentation tasks in microscopy image analysis, such as nuclei instance segmentation in histopathology images [b], cell instance segmentation in electron microscopy images [1].

[a] https://cocodataset.org/#home
[b] A Multi-Organ Nucleus Segmentation Challenge, in IEEE TMI
[1]: the reference #1 in the manuscript.

3) Clarity:

+: The methods and experiment sections are clearly presented.

-: This paper aims at solving the weakly supervised instance segmentation via RL architecture, however, there lack illustrations and discussion on any related instance segmentation works.

4) Significance:

-: Although the authors have presented many experimental results, there still lack experimental results on representative benchmark instance segmentation datasets on general and medical images, which makes me feel that the overall paper lacks significance.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1) The major concern of this work is the lack of experimental results on public benchmark datasets. For the first toy dataset, the background content seems to be fixed (black content and white ‘noise’). For the second microscopy dataset, the morphological structure of the instances is always special: all the instances overlapping together to form a circle-like fruitfly embryo. To this end, the overall experiments on the two datasets are not sufficient to demonstrate the proposed method can be effective on other general datasets, such as the MS COCO. This made the overall impact of the proposed method questionable.  

2) It seems that there are only 10 testing images in the real microscopy dataset, which is a small number. Therefore, a statistical significance test (based on p-value) is suggested for the results on this dataset.

3) Computational complexity analysis is also suggested on the proposed framework.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

In this paper, the authors proposed a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for instance segmentation with superpixels. The proposed framework is based on a stateless actor-critic setup and is demonstrated to be effective on the instance segmentation tasks for one toy dataset and one microscopy dataset.

Main Review:
+: strength
-: weakness 

1)Originality:

In addition, there are also many instance segmentation tasks in microscopy image analysis, such as nuclei instance segmentation in histopathology images [b], cell instance segmentation in electron microscopy images [1].

[b] A Multi-Organ Nucleus Segmentation Challenge, in IEEE TMI

3) Clarity:

Limitations And Societal Impact:

For the first toy dataset, the background content seems to be fixed (black content and white ‘noise’). For the second microscopy dataset, the morphological structure of the instances is always special: all the instances overlapping together to form a circle-like fruitfly embryo. To this end, the overall experiments on the two datasets are not sufficient to demonstrate the proposed method can be effective on other general datasets, such as the MS COCO. This made the overall impact of the proposed method questionable.  

2) It seems that there are only 10 testing images in the real microscopy dataset, which is a small number. Therefore, a statistical significance test (based on p-value) is suggested for the results on this dataset.

3) Computational complexity analysis is also suggested on the proposed framework.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper introduces a novel framework that leverages reinforcement learning (RL) for instance segmentation, utilizing superpixels for weak supervision. The authors implement a stateless actor-critic configuration and provide evidence of its efficacy on a synthetic dataset and a microscopy image collection.

**Main Review:**

**1) Originality:**
*   **Strengths:** The application of an RL-based methodology to tackle weakly supervised instance segmentation, particularly with superpixels, represents a fresh and intriguing direction. This approach distinguishes itself from conventional weakly supervised image segmentation techniques.
*   **Weaknesses:** While the paper adequately covers relevant RL literature, it notably omits a discussion of pertinent works in instance segmentation, encompassing both fully and weakly supervised paradigms.

**2) Quality:**
*   **Strengths:** The effectiveness of the proposed architectural components is substantiated through comprehensive ablation studies conducted on the microscopy dataset. Furthermore, the authors offer a thorough examination of hyper-parameter tuning, including aspects like the number of feature channels and the influence of random seeds.
*   **Weaknesses:** The experimental validation would benefit from more extensive evaluation on widely recognized public instance segmentation benchmarks, such as MS COCO [a]. Additionally, the paper overlooks several significant instance segmentation challenges within microscopy image analysis, for instance, segmenting nuclei in histopathology images [b] or cells in electron microscopy images [1].

**3) Clarity:**
*   **Strengths:** The exposition of the methodology and experimental procedures is commendably clear and well-structured.
*   **Weaknesses:** Despite the central aim of addressing weakly supervised instance segmentation via an RL architecture, the paper lacks illustrative examples or substantial discussion pertaining to existing instance segmentation research.

**4) Significance:**
*   **Weaknesses:** While the paper presents a considerable volume of experimental data, the absence of results from representative benchmark datasets—spanning both general and medical imaging domains—diminishes the overall perceived significance and broader applicability of the work.

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**
1.  A primary concern with this research is the insufficient validation on established public benchmark datasets. The first, synthetic dataset appears to feature a constant background (black content with white 'noise'). The second, microscopy dataset, presents instances with a very specific morphology, where all instances overlap to form a circular fruitfly embryo. Consequently, the experiments performed on these two datasets are not robust enough to convincingly demonstrate the method's potential effectiveness on more diverse, general datasets like MS COCO. This raises questions regarding the proposed method's broader impact.
2.  The real microscopy dataset appears to comprise only 10 test images, which is a rather limited number. Therefore, the inclusion of a statistical significance test (e.g., p-value analysis) for the results obtained on this particular dataset is strongly recommended.
3.  An analysis of the computational complexity of the proposed framework would also be a valuable addition.

**Rating:**
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary:
In this paper, the authors proposed a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for instance segmentation with superpixels. The proposed framework is based on a stateless actor-critic setup and is demonstrated to be effective on the instance segmentation tasks for one toy dataset and one microscopy dataset.

Main Review:
+: strength
-: weakness 

1)Originality: 

+: This paper proposes to solve the instance segmentation tasks with weak supervision (superpixels) using the RL based methods. Different from the typical weakly supervised image instance segmentation methods, the RL-based one is a new and interesting application.

-: The authors only talked about the related work on RL however ignore the related work on instance segmentation (fully supervised and weakly supervised).

-: There lack extensive experiments on public instance segmentation benchmark datasets, such as MS COCO [a]. In addition, there are also many instance segmentation tasks in microscopy image analysis, such as nuclei instance segmentation in histopathology images [b], cell instance segmentation in electron microscopy images [1].

reviews.summary: The paper proposes the use of RL for instance segmentation. In particular, the paper uses super pixels together with graph partitioning and RL to learn the labels of instances. The model is validated on two datasets, one synthetic and one biomedical imaging dataset and is numerically compared to two approaches ([28] and [47]).
reviews.main_review:
**Originality:**

In the paper, the main motivation of the use of RL is to enable optimization of discrete objective. However, this use of RL is standard. Moreover, given this paper motivations, the reviewer would expect to see empirical comparisons to alternative methods to learn discrete assignments  (e.g. straight through, DETR-style https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872, [8]). Thus, from the methodological point of view the novelty is rather limited. From the application point of view, the instance segmentation of light microscopy images, the paper might have some edge of originality; however, it is hard to assess it given the niche domain. To fully appreciate the originality of the method it would be beneficial to validate the approach on a more standard benchmark. 


**Clarity:**

The paper is generally clearly written. However, the clarity of the methods could be improved. The presentation of this section is a bit convoluted making it hard to grasp the overall picture of the proposed approach. The reviewer would encourage the authors to simplify the presentation of the Methods, especially focusing on the aspects of why particular decisions were made. E.g. why are the authors considering method 1 and 2? Why stateless RL with sequence length of 1? Why not use straight through?

For a non-microscopy expert, it is hard to understand the color codings of Figs 4 and 5. 


**Quality and significance:**

The quality and significance of the results are hard to assess given the niche application domain. It would be beneficial to test the ideas on some more standard benchmarks to instance image segmentation (e.g. COCO). 

Additional questions comments:
- Why are superpixels a good prior for this task? 
- Would it be possible to use other priors/partitioning methods?
- It would be nice to discuss the tradeoffs between added computational costs due to the use of RL and metric gains.
- Would it be possible to report the numbers of a standard instance segmentation model for this task, (e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.06870 or some followup)?
